html:
[605]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 明皇自為潞州別駕 - Biographical phrase meaning 'Emperor Xuanzong, when he was serving as the deputy governor of Lüzhou.'
  • 以豪偉聞 - 'Was known for his boldness and grandeur.' *聞* here means 'to be known or famous for.'
  • 與侠少游 - 'Associated with young ruffians or adventurers.' *游* in this context means to associate or befriend.
  • 用此起支庶，踐尊位 - 'Rose to power from a minor branch of the family and reached the highest position.' *支庶* refers to a collateral branch of the royal family, not the direct heir.
  • 穷奢极侈 - Fixed expression meaning 'extremely luxurious and extravagant.'
  • 其阅万方美色众矣 - 'He had seen many beautiful women from all over the world.' *阅* here means 'to have encountered or experienced.'
  • 变易三纲 - 'Altered the three bonds (of Confucian ethics).' *三纲* refers to the Confucian principles of ruler-subject, father-son, and husband-wife relationships.
  • 浊乱四海 - 'Corrupted the whole world.' *四海* is a classical term for the entire world or empire.
  • 思之不少悔 - 'He did not regret it at all.' *少* here is used in the sense of 'a little' or 'in any way.'
  • 是固有以中其心，满其欲矣 - 'This was indeed due to satisfying his heart and fulfilling his desires.' *中其心* means 'to strike the heart' or 'to satisfy the mind.'
  • 江妃者，后先其间 - 'The Jiang妃 was among the concubines before and after [her].' *后先其间* suggests she was among the concubines who came before and after.
  • 以色为所深嫉 - 'She was deeply hated for her beauty.' *为所深嫉* is a passive construction meaning 'was deeply hated.'
  • 议者谓：或覆宗，或非命，均其媚忌自取 - 'Commentators say: Some destroyed their families, others were not destined for it, but all were self-inflicted through flattery and jealousy.' *覆宗* means 'to destroy the family or dynasty.'
  • 殊不知明皇耄而忮忍 - 'Unaware that Emperor Xuanzong, in his old age, became vengeful and cruel.' *耄* means old age, *忮忍* means vengeful and cruel.
  • 至一日杀三子，如轻断蝼蚁之命 - 'To the point of killing three sons in one day, as if cutting off the lives of ants.' *蝼蚁* is a metaphor for insignificant creatures.
  • 奔窜而归，受制昏逆 - 'Fled in panic and returned, subjected to the rule of a corrupt and rebellious person.' *昏逆* refers to a corrupt or rebellious ruler.
  • 四顾嫔嫱，斩亡惧尽 - 'Looking around at his concubines, he feared they would all be executed.' *嫔嫱* refers to the concubines or female attendants.
  • 穷独苟活，天下哀之 - 'Left in isolation, surviving by mere existence, the world pitied him.' *苟活* means to survive in a desperate or degrading way.
  • 《传》曰“以其所不爱及其所爱” - 'As the *Zuo Zhuan* says: 'With the love he did not have, he was repaid with the love he did have.' This is a direct quote from the *Zuo Zhuan* (Commentary of Zuo) or similar classical text.
  • 报复之理，毫发不差 - 'The principle of retribution is not even a hair's breadth off.' *毫发不差* is a fixed expression meaning 'not a single hair's width different.'
  • 是岂特两女子之罪哉！ - 'Is this the fault of only two women?' *岂特* means 'how could it be only,' *哉* is a rhetorical particle.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 明皇 - Refers to Emperor Xuanzong of Tang (712–756 CE), known as Tang Ming Huang. He is famous for his early reign of prosperity and later for his involvement in the An Lushan Rebellion.
  • 潞州别驾 - An official title. *别驾* was a junior official who served as an assistant to the governor of a state or region.
  • 杨氏 - Refers to Yang Guifei, the famous concubine of Emperor Xuanzong. Her name is commonly translated as Yang Guifei or Yang Yuhuan.
  • 三纲 - The Confucian principles of ruler-subject, father-son, and husband-wife relationships. This phrase is central to Confucian moral philosophy.
  • 四海 - A classical term for the entire world or the empire, often used in a poetic or metaphorical sense.
  • 江妃 - Refers to Jiang妃, likely Jiang Qing, one of the concubines of Emperor Xuanzong, though her exact identity may be uncertain or symbolic.
  • 《传》曰 - A reference to a classical text, likely the Zuo Zhuan (Commentary of Zuo), though it could be another historical or philosophical text. The exact source may be uncertain.
  • 覆宗 - To destroy the family or dynasty, a common expression in classical texts to describe the downfall of a ruling house.
  • 昏逆 - Refers to a corrupt or rebellious ruler, often used to describe someone who has lost legitimacy or has become tyrannical.
  • 嫔嫱 - Refers to concubines or female attendants in the imperial court.
  • 苟活 - To survive in a desperate or degrading way, often used to describe someone who is living in shame or hardship.

Proper Nouns

  • 明皇 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang (Tang Ming Huang)
  • 潞州 - Lüzhou, a historical region in present-day Shanxi Province

[606]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 夙根颖异 - Classical phrase meaning 'born with exceptional talent,' often used to describe innate abilities.
  • 授《心经》 - Means 'taught the *Heart Sutra*,' referring to the Buddhist scripture.
  • 一再过了了 - Means 'repeatedly reviewed and understood clearly,' indicating quick comprehension.
  • 覆之，不失一字 - Means 'when reviewed, not a single word was missed,' emphasizing precision and memory.
  • 愿乞作弟子 - Means 'wishes to be made a disciple,' expressing a desire to join a religious order.
  • 即不尔，无令识字，可三十年活耳 - A conditional statement meaning 'if not, do not let her learn to read, for she can only live thirty years,' suggesting a belief in the brevity of life or a curse.
  • 名闺 - Refers to 'famous women' or 'women of high social standing.'
  • 茗战手语 - Refers to 'tea competitions and hand gestures,' likely a metaphor for social gatherings or intellectual contests.
  • 众偶纷然 - Means 'the crowd was in a state of confusion,' indicating a chaotic or lively atmosphere.
  • 悉出意表 - Means 'all were beyond expectation,' describing responses that were surprising or clever.
  • 人人惟恐失姬 - Means 'everyone was afraid of losing her,' indicating her popularity or value.
  • 风期逸艳 - Means 'graceful and charming,' describing her demeanor.
  • 绰约自好 - Means 'graceful and self-assured,' describing her natural elegance.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 虎林 - Likely refers to Hangzhou, a city in Zhejiang Province, known as 'Tiger Lin' in ancient times.
  • 广陵 - Refers to Yangzhou, another city in Jiangsu Province, historically significant for its cultural and economic importance.
  • 《心经》 - The *Heart Sutra*, a central Buddhist text in Mahayana Buddhism.
  • 女塾师 - Refers to a female teacher or educator.
  • 江都 - Refers to Yangzhou, emphasizing its status as a beautiful and culturally rich region.
  • 茗战手语 - Likely a metaphor for social or intellectual gatherings, possibly involving tea ceremonies and gestures.
  • 风期逸艳 - Describes a person's demeanor, combining grace and charm, a common literary trope in describing women of high social standing.

Proper Nouns

  • 小青 - The nickname of the concubine, used throughout the text.
  • 虎林 - Likely Hangzhou, a city in Zhejiang Province.
  • 广陵 - Yangzhou, a city in Jiangsu Province.
  • 《心经》 - The *Heart Sutra*, a Buddhist scripture.
  • 江都 - Another name for Yangzhou, emphasizing its cultural significance.

[607]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 年十六，歸生 - At sixteen, she married [to] Sheng. '歸生' is a classical way of indicating marriage, where '歸' means 'to return' or 'to marry,' and '生' is the husband's name.
  • 豪公子也 - A noble and wealthy young man. '豪公子' is a term used to describe a young man from a wealthy and influential family.
  • 性嘈唼憨跳不韵 - He has a loud, boisterous, and unrefined nature. '嘈唼' describes a noisy and boisterous personality, while '憨跳不韵' suggests a lack of refinement or elegance.
  • 姬曲意下之，終不解 - She tried to please him, but he never understood. '曲意下之' means to go out of one's way to please someone, and '終不解' indicates that despite her efforts, he did not understand her.
  • 隨游天竺 - Accompanied him on a trip to Tianzhu. '天竺' is the ancient Chinese name for India, often used in classical texts.
  • 吾聞西方佛無量，而世多尊禮大士者何 - I have heard that there are countless Buddhas in the West, but why is the world so devoted to venerating the Bodhisattva? '大士' refers to a Bodhisattva, a being who is on the path to Buddhahood but delays their own enlightenment to help others.
  • 以其慈悲耳 - Because of their compassion. '慈悲' means compassion or mercy, a central concept in Buddhism.
  • 吾當慈悲汝 - I will show compassion to you. This is a sarcastic statement, implying that the woman will 'show compassion' by punishing the man.
  • 徙之孤山別業 - Moved her to a separate residence in Solitary Mountain. '孤山' refers to a specific mountain, likely in the context of the story.
  • 非吾命而郎至，不得入！非吾命而郎手札至，亦不得入！ - Without my permission, if my husband comes, you may not enter! Without my permission, if my husband's letter comes, you may not enter! This is a strict rule imposed by the woman.
  • 姬自念彼置我閒地，必密伺短長，借莫須有事魚肉我，以故深自敛戢 - She thought that he had placed her in a secluded place, and he must be secretly watching for her faults, fabricating false charges to oppress her, so she deeply restrained herself. '閒地' means a secluded or isolated place, '密伺短長' means to secretly watch for faults, and '魚肉我' means to oppress or torment her like fish or meat.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 天竺 - The ancient Chinese name for India, often used in classical texts to refer to the land of Buddhism.
  • 大士 - A Bodhisattva, a being who is on the path to Buddhahood but delays their own enlightenment to help others.
  • 孤山 - A specific mountain, likely in the context of the story, possibly referring to a real or fictional location.
  • 游冶少年 - Refers to young men who are playful, flirtatious, and possibly of lower social status.
  • 諸女伴 - Refers to the other women accompanying the main character, likely of similar social status.

Proper Nouns

  • 姬 - The main character, a woman.
  • 生 - The husband, a young man from a wealthy family.
  • 孤山 - A specific mountain, likely in the context of the story.
  • 天竺 - The ancient Chinese name for India.

[608]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 戚属 - Refers to relatives or close kin.
  • 才而贤 - Means 'talented and virtuous.'
  • 学奕 - Means 'to learn chess' or 'to study chess.'
  • 巨觞觞妇 - Means 'to offer a large cup of wine to the woman.'
  • 瞷妇已醉 - Means 'to see that the woman is already drunk.'
  • 徐语姬曰 - Means 'to speak to the concubine slowly.'
  • 船有楼，汝伴我一登 - Means 'There is a tower on the boat; you should accompany me to climb it.'
  • 远眺久之 - Means 'to look far into the distance for a long time.'
  • 抚姬背曰 - Means 'to pat the concubine's back and say.'
  • 好光景，可惜 - Means 'Such a beautiful scene, it's a pity.'
  • 无自苦 - Means 'do not make yourself suffer.'
  • 子作蒲团空观邪 - Means 'Are you sitting in meditation in vain?'
  • 贾平章剑锋可畏也 - Means 'The sword of General Jia is to be feared.'
  • 子误矣 - Means 'You are mistaken.'
  • 平章剑钝，女平章乃利害耳 - Means 'General Jia's sword is blunt, but the female General Jia is dangerous.'
  • 顾左右寂无人 - Means 'Looking around, there is no one else.'
  • 从容讽曰 - Means 'to speak gently and subtly.'
  • 子才韵色艺无双 - Means 'Your talent, grace, appearance, and artistry are unparalleled.'
  • 岂当堕罗刹国中 - Means 'How can you fall into the realm of demons?'
  • 吾虽非女侠，力能脱子火坑 - Means 'Although I am not a female hero, I have the strength to save you from the fire pit.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 戚属 - Refers to relatives or close kin.
  • 才而贤 - A common phrase used to describe someone who is both talented and virtuous.
  • 学奕 - Chess (象棋) was a popular game in ancient China and often used as a metaphor for strategic thinking.
  • 巨觞觞妇 - Offering wine was a common social gesture in ancient China, often used to show hospitality or affection.
  • 瞷妇已醉 - Observing someone's state of intoxication was a common practice in social settings.
  • 船有楼，汝伴我一登 - Boats with towers were luxurious and often used for leisure activities.
  • 远眺久之 - Looking far into the distance was a common activity for relaxation and reflection.
  • 抚姬背曰 - Patting someone's back was a gesture of comfort or reassurance.
  • 好光景，可惜 - A common expression of regret or appreciation for a beautiful scene.
  • 无自苦 - A gentle reminder to take care of oneself.
  • 章台柳亦倚红楼盼韩郎走马 - This allusion comes from a famous Tang Dynasty poem by Wang Changling, where 'Zhangtai Willow' represents a courtesan waiting for her lover.
  • 子作蒲团空观邪 - Refers to Buddhist meditation practices, where one sits in a lotus position.
  • 贾平章剑锋可畏也 - General Jia Ping was a historical figure known for his military prowess.
  • 子误矣 - A common phrase used to correct someone's mistake.
  • 平章剑钝，女平章乃利害耳 - A play on words, where '平章' (General Jia) is contrasted with '女平章' (female General Jia).
  • 顾左右寂无人 - A common phrase used to indicate that one is alone.
  • 从容讽曰 - A gentle and subtle way of speaking, often used in diplomatic or advisory contexts.
  • 子才韵色艺无双 - A common phrase used to praise someone's exceptional qualities.
  • 岂当堕罗刹国中 - A metaphorical expression, where '罗刹国' (realm of demons) represents a dangerous or undesirable situation.
  • 吾虽非女侠，力能脱子火坑 - A metaphorical expression, where '火坑' (fire pit) represents a dangerous or difficult situation.

Proper Nouns

  • 某夫人 - A specific woman, but her name is not provided.
  • 姬 - Refers to the concubine.
  • 贾平章 - A historical figure known as General Jia Ping.
  • 章台柳 - A metaphorical reference to a courtesan.
  • 韩郎 - A metaphorical reference to a lover.
  • 蒲团 - A type of cushion used in meditation.
  • 罗刹国 - A metaphorical reference to a dangerous or undesirable situation.
  • 火坑 - A metaphorical reference to a dangerous or difficult situation.

[609]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 顷言章台事 - Colloquial expression meaning 'recently discussing the matter of the Zhangtai.'
  • 子非会心人邪 - Rhetorical question meaning 'you are not someone who understands the situation?'
  • 拔一眼中钉耳 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to remove a thorn from one's eye,' suggesting relief or resolution.
  • 纵能容子 - Concessive phrase meaning 'even if he could tolerate you.'
  • 党将军帐下作羔酒侍儿乎 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to serve as a concubine or attendant in the general's tent,' implying a subordinate or servile role.
  • 夙孽未了 - Phrase meaning 'past sins or misfortunes have not been resolved.'
  • 冥曹姻缘簿 - Reference to the 'Book of Marriages in the Underworld,' a concept from Chinese folklore.
  • 非吾如意珠 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'not my ideal or perfect match.'
  • 徒供群口画描耳 - Phrase meaning 'only to be subject to the idle talk and gossip of others.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 章台 - Refers to the Zhangtai, a historical place or event.
  • 韩君平 - Reference to a historical figure or a character in a story.
  • 党将军 - Refers to a general named 'Dang,' likely a historical or fictional figure.
  • 冥曹 - Refers to the underworld or the realm of the dead in Chinese folklore.
  • 姻缘簿 - Refers to the 'Book of Marriages in the Underworld,' a concept from Chinese folklore.

Proper Nouns

  • 章台 - A historical place or event.
  • 韩君平 - A historical figure or character in a story.
  • 党将军 - A general named 'Dang,' likely a historical or fictional figure.
  • 冥曹 - The underworld or the realm of the dead in Chinese folklore.
  • 姻缘簿 - The 'Book of Marriages in the Underworld,' a concept from Chinese folklore.

[610]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 幽愤凄怨 - Describes a deep sense of sorrow, resentment, and melancholy.
  • 俱托之诗或小词 - Means 'she entrusted all her feelings to poetry or short lyrics.'
  • 无与同调者 - Means 'no one to share her sentiments with.'
  • 郁郁感疾 - Describes a state of deep depression leading to illness.
  • 岁余益深 - Means 'the illness deepened over the years.'
  • 妇命医来 - Means 'the lady ordered a doctor to come.'
  • 仍遣婢以药至 - Means 'she still sent a maid with medicine.'
  • 姬佯感谢 - Means 'the lady pretended to be grateful.'
  • 婢出，掷药床头 - Means 'when the maid left, she threw the medicine onto the bed.'
  • 吾固不愿生 - Means 'I certainly don’t wish to live.'
  • 亦当以净体皈依 - Means 'I should return to purity and devote myself.'
  • 作刘安鸡犬 - Refers to the legend of Liu An, who transformed his dog and chicken into immortals.
  • 岂汝一杯鸩能断送乎 - Means 'Could your single cup of poison destroy me?'
  • 病益不支 - Means 'the illness became increasingly unbearable.'
  • 水粒俱绝 - Means 'she stopped eating and drinking altogether.'
  • 日饮梨汁一小盏许 - Means 'she drank only a small cup of pear juice each day.'
  • 益明妆冶服 - Means 'she dressed even more elaborately.'
  • 拥襟欹坐 - Means 'she sat with her sleeves gathered around her, leaning slightly.'
  • 或呼琵琶妇唱盲词自遣 - Means 'she sometimes called a lute player to sing blind lyrics to distract herself.'
  • 数晕数醒 - Means 'she would faint and wake up repeatedly.'
  • 终不蓬首偃卧也 - Means 'but she never let her hair become disheveled or lay down to sleep.'
  • 可传语冤孽郎，觅一良画师来 - Means 'she could tell her unfaithful husband to find a good painter.'
  • 师至，命写照 - Means 'when the painter arrived, she ordered a portrait to be drawn.'
  • 写毕，揽镜熟视 - Means 'after it was finished, she took the mirror and looked at it carefully.'
  • 得吾形似矣，未尽吾神也 - Means 'the shape is like me, but it doesn’t capture my spirit.'
  • 姑置之 - Means 'she set it aside temporarily.'
  • 又易一图 - Means 'she had another one drawn.'
  • 神是矣，而风态未流动也 - Means 'the spirit is there, but the grace and vitality are not captured.'
  • 若见我而目端手庄，太矜持故也 - Means 'If you see me and your eyes and hands are too stiff, it’s because you’re too formal.'
  • 命捉笔于傍，而自与妪指顾语笑 - Means 'she ordered the painter to hold the brush and she gestured and laughed with the old woman.'
  • 或扇茶铛，或简书，或自整衣褶，或代调丹璧诸色 - Means 'she fanned the tea pot, or held up books, or adjusted her clothes, or helped mix the colors of the pigments.'
  • 纵其想会 - Means 'letting her imagination flow.'
  • 须臾图成，果极妖纤之致 - Means 'in a short while, the painting was completed, truly capturing her delicate and beautiful essence.'
  • 笑曰：“可矣！” - Means 'she smiled and said, 'It’s perfect!''
  • 师去，取图供榻前，焚香设梨酒奠之 - Means 'when the painter left, she took the painting to her bed, lit incense, and set a pear juice offering.'
  • 小青，小青，此中岂有汝缘兮乎？ - Means 'Xiao Qing, Xiao Qing, is there any fate for you here?'
  • 抚几，泪潸潸如雨，一恸而绝 - Means 'she touched the table, tears streaming down her face like rain, and died in a fit of grief.'
  • 时年十八耳 - Means 'she was only eighteen years old.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 姬 - Refers to a lady, likely a noblewoman.
  • 夫人后 - Refers to the lady’s wife, who was also a noblewoman.
  • 宦远方 - Refers to being sent to a distant place for official duties.
  • 刘安鸡犬 - Refers to the legend of Liu An, a Han dynasty prince who, according to legend, transformed his dog and chicken into immortals.
  • 琵琶妇唱盲词 - Refers to a lute player singing blind lyrics, a form of traditional Chinese music and storytelling.
  • 小青 - Likely a reference to a historical or literary figure, possibly a concubine or a fictional character.

Proper Nouns

  • 姬 - A lady, likely a noblewoman.
  • 夫人后 - The lady’s wife, also a noblewoman.
  • 刘安 - A Han dynasty prince known for the legend of transforming his dog and chicken into immortals.
  • 小青 - Likely a reference to a historical or literary figure, possibly a concubine or a fictional character.

[611]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 日向暮 - Literally means 'the sun approaches dusk,' used to indicate the time of day.
  • 踉跄 - Describes a stumbling or unsteady gait, often due to fatigue or distress.
  • 披帷 - Means 'to pull aside the curtain,' indicating the act of entering or revealing something.
  • 容光藻逸 - Describes a radiant and elegant appearance, often used to depict someone's beauty or grace.
  • 衣态鲜好 - Means 'clothes are fresh and good,' indicating well-maintained and attractive attire.
  • 长号顿足 - Describes loud weeping and stamping of feet, indicating intense grief or sorrow.
  • 呕血升余 - Means 'vomit blood more than a sheng,' indicating severe illness or emotional distress.
  • 叙致惋痛 - Means 'expressing regret and pain,' describing the content of the letter.
  • 立焚之 - Means 'burn it immediately,' indicating the act of destroying something quickly.
  • 草稿 - Refers to a rough draft or initial version of a written work.
  • 花钿数事 - Refers to several pieces of decorative jewelry, often used as a gift.
  • 衬以二纸 - Means 'accompanied by two sheets of paper,' indicating additional material.
  • 古诗 - Refers to an ancient poem, typically longer and more formal than a verse.
  • 绝句 - Refers to a short poem, typically consisting of four lines.
  • 词 - Refers to a type of lyrical poetry, often set to music.
  • 雪意阁云云不流 - Describes a scene where clouds are heavy and immobile, suggesting a cold and still atmosphere.
  • 旧云正压新云头 - Means 'old clouds are pressing down on new clouds,' indicating a sense of pressure or oppression.
  • 米颠颠笔落窗外 - Refers to the eccentric behavior of Mi Fu, a famous calligrapher, whose brush strokes seem to fall outside the window, suggesting a free and unrestrained style.
  • 松岚秀处当我楼 - Means 'the beautiful mist of pine trees meets my tower,' indicating a serene and picturesque setting.
  • 垂帘只愁好景少 - Means 'hanging the curtain only worries about the few good scenes,' expressing a desire to enjoy the beauty of the surroundings.
  • 卷帘又怕风缭绕 - Means 'rolling up the curtain fears the wind will be turbulent,' expressing a concern about the wind disrupting the peace.
  • 帘卷帘垂底事难 - Means 'rolling up and hanging down the curtain, the matter is difficult,' indicating the complexity of the situation.
  • 不情不绪谁能晓 - Means 'no emotion, no mood, who can understand,' expressing a state of emotional detachment.
  • 炉烟渐瘐剪声小 - Means 'the smoke from the stove gradually diminishes, the sound of the scissors becomes small,' indicating a quiet and peaceful atmosphere.
  • 孤鸿唳悄悄 - Means 'a solitary goose cries quietly,' suggesting loneliness and melancholy.
  • 稽首慈云大士前 - Means 'bowing deeply before the Great Bodhisattva of Compassion,' indicating a gesture of reverence.
  • 莫生西土莫生天 - Means 'do not be born in the West, do not be born in the sky,' expressing a wish to avoid certain places or states.
  • 愿为一滴杨枝水 - Means 'wishing to be a drop of water from the willow branch,' indicating a desire to help others.
  • 洒作人间并蒂莲 - Means 'to pour out and become twin lotuses in the world,' suggesting a wish for unity and harmony.
  • 春衫血泪点轻纱 - Means 'spring clothes stained with blood and tears on light gauze,' indicating sorrow and loss.
  • 吹入林逋处士家 - Means 'blown into the house of the recluse Lin Bu,' indicating a place of solitude and seclusion.
  • 岭上梅花三百树 - Means 'three hundred plum trees on the mountain,' indicating a large number of plum trees.
  • 一时应变杜鹃花 - Means 'at once transformed into rhododendrons,' suggesting a change in appearance or state.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 日向暮 - Refers to the time of day, specifically dusk.
  • 踉跄 - A common term used to describe a person's unsteady movement, often due to fatigue or distress.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 新妝竟與畫圖爭 - Literally means 'new makeup competes with painted portraits,' suggesting a comparison between the beauty of a woman and that of a painting.
  • 知在昭陽第几名 - Ambiguous phrase; could mean 'knowing her rank among the beauties of the palace' or 'knowing her position in the harem.' '昭阳' refers to a palace in the Han dynasty, often associated with the harem.
  • 瘦影自臨春水照 - '瘦影' means 'slender shadow,' and '自臨春水照' means 'by herself facing the spring water for reflection.' Suggests a solitary and introspective moment.
  • 卿須憐我我憐卿 - Reciprocal expression of mutual affection, meaning 'you must love me, and I will love you.'
  • 西陵芳草騎辚辚 - '西陵' refers to a place, possibly a burial ground, and '芳草' means 'fragrant grass.' '骑辚辚' describes the sound of wheels rolling, suggesting a journey or travel.
  • 內信傳來喚踏春 - '内信' means 'internal message' or 'message from inside,' and '唤踏春' means 'calling out to step into spring.' Suggests a call to enjoy the season.
  • 杯酒自浇苏小墓 - '杯酒' means 'a cup of wine,' '自浇' means 'pouring by oneself,' and '苏小墓' refers to the grave of Su Xiaoxiao, a famous courtesan from the Southern Song dynasty. Suggests a personal act of remembrance or tribute.
  • 可知妾是意中人 - '妾' means 'I' (used by a woman), and '意中人' means 'the one in my heart.' Means 'do you know that I am the one in your heart?'
  • 冷雨幽窗不可聽 - '冷雨' means 'cold rain,' '幽窗' means 'secluded window,' and '不可聽' means 'cannot be listened to.' Suggests a melancholic and isolated atmosphere.
  • 挑燈間看《牡丹亭》 - '挑燈' means 'to light a lamp,' '間' means 'in between,' and '看《牡丹亭》' means 'to read or watch *The Peony Pavilion*.' Suggests a quiet, intimate moment of reading or watching a play.
  • 人間亦有痴於我 - '人間' means 'in the world,' '亦有' means 'also has,' '痴於我' means 'more foolish than me.' Suggests that there are people in the world who are even more devoted or infatuated than the speaker.
  • 豈獨傷心是小青 - '豈獨' means 'not only,' '傷心' means 'heartbroken,' and '小青' refers to Xiao Qing, a famous courtesan from the Southern Song dynasty. Means 'not only is Xiao Qing heartbroken.'
  • 何處雙禽集畫闌 - '何處' means 'where,' '雙禽' means 'two birds,' '集' means 'gather,' and '畫闌' means 'painted railing.' Suggests a scene of two birds gathering at a painted railing.
  • 朱朱翠翠似青鸞 - '朱朱翠翠' means 'red and green,' and '似青鸞' means 'like a blue phoenix.' Suggests a colorful and beautiful scene.
  • 如今幾個憐文彩 - '如今' means 'now,' '幾個' means 'how many,' '憐' means 'to pity or love,' and '文彩' means 'literary talent.' Means 'how many people now love or appreciate literary talent?'
  • 也向秋風鬥羽翰 - '也向' means 'also in,' '秋風' means 'autumn wind,' '鬥' means 'to compete,' and '羽翰' means 'feathers.' Suggests a competition or struggle in the autumn wind.
  • 脈脈溶溶艷艷波 - '脈脈' means 'quietly and deeply,' '溶溶' means 'smoothly and gently,' and '艷艷波' means 'beautiful waves.' Describes a serene and beautiful scene.
  • 芙蓉睡醒欲如何 - '芙蓉' means 'lotus,' '睡醒' means 'awake from sleep,' and '欲如何' means 'what to do.' Suggests a lotus flower waking up and wondering what to do next.
  • 妾映鏡中花映水 - '妾' means 'I' (used by a woman), '映鏡' means 'reflected in the mirror,' '花映水' means 'flowers reflected in water.' Suggests a woman seeing her reflection in the mirror, with flowers reflected in the water.
  • 不知秋思落誰多 - '不知' means 'do not know,' '秋思' means 'autumn thoughts,' and '落誰多' means 'fall on whom more.' Means 'do not know whose autumn thoughts fall more heavily.'
  • 盈盈金谷女班頭 - '盈盈' means 'graceful and charming,' '金谷' refers to a place, possibly a garden or a palace, and '女班頭' means 'the leader of the women.' Suggests a graceful and charming woman who is the leader of the women.
  • 一曲驪珠眾伎收 - '一曲' means 'a song,' '驪珠' means 'pearl from a horse's hoof,' and '眾伎收' means 'collected by many performers.' Suggests that a song is highly valued and collected by many performers.
  • 直得樓前身一死 - '直得' means 'worth,' '樓前' means 'in front of the building,' and '身一死' means 'to die.' Means 'it is worth dying in front of the building.'
  • 季倫原是解風流 - '季倫' refers to Shi Chong, a famous figure from the Jin dynasty, and '原是解風流' means 'originally understood elegance and charm.' Suggests that Shi Chong was known for his elegance and charm.
  • 鄉心不畏兩峰高 - '鄉心' means 'hometown heart,' '不畏' means 'not afraid,' and '兩峰高' means 'two high peaks.' Means 'the hometown heart is not afraid of two high peaks.'
  • 昨夜慈親入夢遙 - '昨夜' means 'last night,' '慈親' means 'loving parents,' and '入夢遙' means 'into a distant dream.' Suggests that the speaker's loving parents appeared in a distant dream last night.
  • 說是浙江潮有信 - '說是' means 'it is said,' '浙江潮' means 'the tide of Zhejiang,' and '有信' means 'is reliable.' Means 'it is said that the tide of Zhejiang is reliable.'
  • 浙潮爭似廣陵潮 - '浙潮' means 'the tide of Zhejiang,' '爭似' means 'how can compare to,' and '廣陵潮' means 'the tide of Guangling.' Means 'how can the tide of Zhejiang compare to the tide of Guangling?'
  • 文姬遠嫁昭君塞 - '文姬' refers to Cai Wenji, a famous woman from the Han dynasty, and '遠嫁昭君塞' means 'married far away to the territory of Zhaojun.' Suggests that Cai Wenji was married far away to the territory of Zhaojun.
  • 小青又續風流債 - '小青' refers to Xiao Qing, a famous courtesan from the Southern Song dynasty, and '又續風流債' means 'continuing the debt of romance.' Suggests that Xiao Qing continued her romantic affairs.
  • 也虧一陣黑罡風 - '也虧' means 'thanks to,' '一陣' means 'a gust,' '黑罡風' means 'black gale wind.' Suggests that thanks to a gust of black gale wind.
  • 原不是鴛鴦一派 - '原不是' means 'originally not,' '鴛鴦一派' means 'a lineage of mandarin ducks.' Means 'originally not part of the lineage of mandarin ducks.'
  • 休算做相思一（上既下木） - '休算做' means 'do not count as,' '相思' means 'yearning,' and '一（上既下木）' is a rare character that may refer to a specific type of tree or plant. Means 'do not count as yearning for a specific type of tree or plant.'
  • 自思自解自商量 - '自思' means 'think by oneself,' '自解' means 'solve by oneself,' and '自商量' means 'discuss by oneself.' Suggests thinking, solving, and discussing by oneself.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 憾促欢淹 - Means 'regretting the short-lived joy,' expressing sorrow over the brevity of happiness.

  • 夜台滋味，谅不殊斯 - Means 'the taste of the night world is surely not different from this,' suggesting that death is not much different from life.

  • 轩车南返，驻节维扬 - Means 'returning south in a carriage, stopping at Weiyang,' indicating a journey or return.
  • 老母惠存，如妾之受 - Means 'the kind care of my mother, as I have received,' expressing gratitude.
  • 阿秦可念，幸终垂悯 - Means 'A Qin is worthy of remembrance, may you always show compassion,' expressing a plea for continued kindness.
  • 畴昔珍赠，悉令见殉 - Means 'all the precious gifts from the past, all have been sacrificed,' indicating a sense of loss.
  • 宝钿绣衣，福星所赐 - Means 'jeweled hairpins and embroidered clothes, bestowed by the star of good fortune,' indicating items of great value and significance.
  • 可以超轮消劫耳 - Means 'they can transcend the cycle and eliminate calamity,' suggesting a belief in the power of these items to bring about positive change.
  • 小六娘竟先期相俟 - Means 'Little Sixth Miss has already waited in advance,' indicating that someone is ready to accompany the speaker.

  • 其诗集、小像，托陈媪好藏，觅便驰寄 - Means 'her poetry collection and portrait, entrusted to Aunt Chen to keep well, to be sent as soon as possible,' indicating a request for safekeeping and delivery.
  • 身不自保，何有于零膏冷翠乎 - Means 'if I cannot even protect myself, what do I have to do with cold, lifeless beauty?' expressing a sense of futility.
  • 他时放船堤下，探梅山中，开我西阁门，坐我绿阴床 - Means 'some day, when I release the boat under the embankment, explore the plum blossoms in the mountains, open my western pavilion door, and sit on my green shade bed,' indicating a desire for future peace and tranquility.
  • 髣生平于响像，见空帏之寂飏 - Means 'to see my life reflected in echoes, to see the empty curtain fluttering in silence,' expressing a sense of nostalgia and loneliness.
  • 是邪非邪，其人斯在 - Means 'is it true or not, the person is still here,' expressing uncertainty and longing.

  • 但微见眉痕惨然，似有泣意 - 'But only slightly see the sad trace on her eyebrows, as if she is about to cry,' describing a subtle expression of sorrow.

  • 倩女魂 - A reference to a beautiful and ethereal spirit, often used in classical literature to describe a graceful and tragic figure.
  • 秋海棠花 - A specific type of flower known for its beauty and delicacy, often used in poetry to evoke a sense of elegance and sorrow.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 不知何许人 - Common phrase meaning 'unknown person' or 'unknown origin.'
  • 其詩與叙，見於心甲驿壁 - Indicates that the poem and narrative were found on the wall of the Xinxia station.
  • 文叙云 - Means 'the narrative says' or 'the text states.'
  • 予生长会稽 - Means 'I was born and raised in Kuaiji' (modern-day Shaoxing, Zhejiang).
  • 幼工书史 - Means 'I was skilled in literature and history from a young age.'
  • 年方及笄 - Refers to the age of a girl when she has her coming-of-age ceremony (around 15 years old).
  • 嫁于燕客 - Means 'married to a guest from Yan' (Yan was an ancient state in northern China).
  • 具林下之风致 - Means 'possessing the grace of a hermit in the woods.'
  • 事腹负之将军 - Means 'serving a general who is overweight.'
  • 加以河东狮子，日吼数声 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'furthermore, the lion from Hedong roars several times a day,' referring to a nagging and domineering wife.
  • 今早‘薄言往诉，逢彼之怒’ - Reference to the poem '薄言往诉' from the Book of Songs, meaning 'I went to complain, but met with his anger.'
  • 鞭挞乱下，辱等奴婢 - Means 'he beat me mercilessly, treating me like a slave.'
  • 气填胸臆，几不能起 - Means 'my heart was filled with anger, and I almost couldn't stand up.'
  • 嗟乎！予笼中人耳，死何足惜 - Means 'Alas! I am just a caged bird; my death is not worth mentioning.'
  • 但恐委身草莽，湮没无闻 - Means 'but I fear that if I die, I will be forgotten and lost in obscurity.'
  • 故忍死须臾，俟同类睡熟，窃至后庭，以泪和墨，题三诗于壁 - Means 'so I endured a moment of death, waiting until my companions were asleep, and secretly went to the back courtyard, mixing my tears with ink to write three poems on the wall.'
  • 庶知音者读之，悲予生之不辰，则予死且不朽 - Means 'hoping that those who understand will read them, pitying my misfortune in life, so that even in death, I will be remembered.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 驿亭 - Refers to a post station or rest stop along ancient Chinese roads.
  • 心甲驿 - The name of a specific post station.
  • 会稽 - An ancient name for the region around modern-day Shaoxing, Zhejiang.
  • 燕客 - Refers to a guest from Yan, an ancient state in northern China.
  • 河东狮子 - A metaphor for a nagging and domineering wife, derived from the story of a man whose wife was known for her loud and constant complaints.
  • 薄言往诉 - A line from the Book of Songs, used here to express the woman's attempt to complain to her husband.
  • 草莽 - Refers to wilderness or uncultivated land, often used metaphorically to mean obscurity or oblivion.

[618]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 古驿无情恣客游 - Poetic expression meaning 'the ancient inn, indifferent, allows travelers to wander freely.' Combines the imagery of an ancient inn with the idea of unrestricted travel.
  • 悲悲喜喜任悠悠 - Rhythmic phrase meaning 'sorrow and joy, left to drift aimlessly.' Conveys a sense of emotional ebb and flow without restraint.
  • 粉墙难比生公石 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'the whitewashed wall cannot compare to the living stone of Sheng Gong.' Likely refers to the enduring nature of stone compared to the transient nature of painted walls.
  • 诉尽衷肠不点头 - Phrase meaning 'having poured out one's heart, it does not nod in agreement.' Suggests a sense of unreciprocated emotion or understanding.
  • 已嫁从夫怨阿谁 - Rhetorical question meaning 'having married and followed her husband, to whom can she complain?' Reflects the traditional role of a wife in ancient Chinese society.
  • 换花换马亦何悲 - Phrase meaning 'even exchanging flowers and horses, what is there to mourn?' Suggests that material exchanges are trivial compared to deeper emotional or moral issues.
  • 忍将无限闺中苦 - Phrase meaning 'how can one bear the endless suffering of the inner chambers?' Refers to the hardships endured by women in the private, domestic sphere.
  • 换取诗名壁上垂 - Phrase meaning 'to exchange for a poetic reputation hanging on the wall.' Suggests that fame or recognition can be achieved through poetry.
  • 鸦凤相欺今恨谁 - Rhetorical question meaning 'now whom does the crow and phoenix deceive?' May refer to a situation where the innocent are wronged by the powerful or the deceitful.
  • 来生猫鼠转欢悲 - Phrase meaning 'in the next life, the cat and mouse will alternate between joy and sorrow.' Suggests a cycle of fate or karma.
  • 我修妒史书卿句 - Phrase meaning 'I revise the history of jealousy and write your words.' Suggests a reflection on the nature of jealousy and its portrayal in literature.
  • 翻喜才名为妒垂 - Phrase meaning 'instead, I rejoice that talent and name are overshadowed by jealousy.' Suggests a paradoxical appreciation for the role of jealousy in shaping one's reputation.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 古驿 - Refers to an ancient inn or驿站 (yì zhàn), a place where travelers would rest. Evokes a sense of timelessness and the transient nature of human experience.
  • 生公石 - Refers to the famous stone of Sheng Gong, a Buddhist monk known for his teachings. The stone is often associated with enduring wisdom or enlightenment.
  • 修孊夫人 - Historical figure, the wife of King Guangchuan of the Han dynasty. Known for her jealousy and cruelty, particularly in her treatment of the concubine Wang Qing.
  • 广川王去 - Refers to King Guangchuan of the Han dynasty, who was known for his cruel treatment of his concubines.
  • 阳城昭信 - A concubine of King Guangchuan who was favored by the king and eventually became his wife after the death of his original wife.
  • 望卿 - A concubine of King Guangchuan who was cruelly treated and eventually killed by the king and his wife.
  • 缯帛 - Refers to silk fabrics, which were valuable and often used as gifts or rewards in ancient China.

Proper Nouns

  • 修孊夫人 - The historical figure known as the wife of King Guangchuan of the Han dynasty.
  • 广川王去 - King Guangchuan of the Han dynasty.
  • 阳城昭信 - A concubine of King Guangchuan who became his wife.
  • 望卿 - A concubine of King Guangchuan who was cruelly treated and killed.

  • 君臣同志、华夷同风 - A poetic phrase meaning 'the ruler and subjects share the same aspirations, and the Chinese and foreigners share the same customs.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 扫深殿 - Sweep the deep hall, a metaphor for preparing a space or a state of readiness.
  • 闭久金铺暗 - The golden plaques grow dark from long closure, describing the neglect or abandonment of a place.
  • 游丝络纲尘作堆 - Silk threads and dust form piles, a vivid description of disarray and neglect.
  • 积岁青苔厚阶面 - Years of moss have thickened the steps, indicating long-term disuse.
  • 拂象床 - Wipe the ivory bed, a metaphor for preparing a place for someone's arrival.
  • 凭梦借高唐 - Rely on dreams to borrow from the high tower, a reference to the legend of Cui Yingying and Zhang Sheng.
  • 敲坏牛边知妾卧 - Knock on the cowshed to know where the concubine sleeps, a metaphor for checking on someone's whereabouts.
  • 恰当天处少辉光 - Just at the place where there is little light, indicating a dim or gloomy setting.
  • 换香枕 - Change the fragrant pillow, a metaphor for preparing a place for rest.
  • 一半无云锦 - Half without cloud-patterned brocade, describing a bedspread that is incomplete or worn.
  • 为是秋来辗转多 - Because of the many turns in autumn, indicating the restlessness and discomfort of the season.
  • 更有双双泪痕渗 - There are also double tear marks seeping through, suggesting emotional distress.
  • 铺翠被 - Spread the green quilt, a metaphor for preparing a place for sleep.
  • 羞杀鸳鸯对 - Shame the paired mandarin ducks, a reference to the loyalty and fidelity of mandarin ducks, often used in love poetry.
  • 犹忆当时叫合欢 - Still remember when we called for the joyous reunion, a nostalgic reference to a happy time.
  • 而今独覆相思块 - Now alone covering the block of longing, indicating the current state of loneliness and longing.
  • 装绣帐 - Set up the embroidered canopy, a metaphor for preparing a place for sleep or rest.
  • 金钩未敢上 - The golden hook is not dared to be lifted, indicating hesitation or fear.
  • 解却四角夜光珠 - Unfasten the four-cornered night-light pearls, a metaphor for removing the light sources.
  • 不教照见愁模样 - So as not to show the sorrowful face, indicating a desire to hide one's distress.
  • 叠锦茵 - Fold the brocade mat, a metaphor for preparing a place for someone to sit or lie down.
  • 只愿身当白玉体 - Only wish to be with the body of white jade, a metaphor for desiring a pure and beautiful partner.
  • 不愿伊当薄命人 - Do not wish for you to be a person of thin fate, expressing a wish for the other person to have a good life.
  • 展瑶席 - Unfold the jade mat, a metaphor for preparing a place for rest.
  • 花笑三韩碧 - Flowers smile in the green of the Three Han, a reference to the beauty of nature.
  • 笑妾新铺玉一床 - Laugh at me for newly laying out a bed of jade, indicating a sense of pride or self-deprecation.
  • 从来妇欢不终夕 - From the beginning, the joy of the wife does not last the whole night, a reference to the fleeting nature of happiness.
  • 剔银灯 - Trim the silver lamp, a metaphor for preparing a place for someone to stay.
  • 须知一样明 - Need to know that it is equally bright, indicating that the light is the same regardless of the situation.
  • 偏是君来生彩晕 - Especially when you come, it produces a colorful glow, indicating a special effect when the person arrives.
  • 对妾故作青荧荧 - Facing me, it intentionally makes a greenish glow, indicating a deliberate action to create a specific atmosphere.
  • 待君行 - Awaiting your departure, indicating a sense of anticipation or farewell.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 扫深殿，闭久金铺暗 - Refers to the neglect and abandonment of a palace, possibly alluding to the decline of a dynasty.
  • 游丝络纲尘作堆，积岁青苔厚阶面 - Describes the decay and disuse of a place, often used in literature to evoke a sense of time passing and the impermanence of power.
  • 拂象床，凭梦借高唐 - References the legend of Cui Yingying and Zhang Sheng, where Cui Yingying's dream takes her to the high tower, symbolizing a romantic or idealized state.
  • 敲坏牛边知妾卧，恰当天处少辉光 - Uses the metaphor of checking on someone's whereabouts and the dim lighting to create a sense of isolation and secrecy.
  • 换香枕，一半无云锦 - Describes a bedspread that is incomplete or worn, often used to symbolize the passage of time and the fading of youth.
  • 为是秋来辗转多，更有双双泪痕渗 - Evokes the restlessness and emotional distress of autumn, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
  • 铺翠被，羞杀鸳鸯对 - Uses the metaphor of mandarin ducks to symbolize loyalty and fidelity, often used in love poetry.
  • 犹忆当时叫合欢，而今独覆相思块 - Nostalgically recalls a happy time and contrasts it with the current state of loneliness and longing.
  • 装绣帐，金钩未敢上 - Describes the hesitation or fear of removing the light sources, often used to create a sense of anticipation or unease.
  • 解却四角夜光珠，不教照见愁模样 - Uses the metaphor of removing light sources to hide one's distress, often used to express a desire for privacy or concealment.
  • 不愿伊当薄命人 - Wishes for the other person to have a good life, often used to express concern for the other person's well-being.
  • 展瑶席，花笑三韩碧 - Uses the beauty of nature to create a sense of joy and contentment.
  • 笑妾新铺玉一床，从来妇欢不终夕 - Expresses a sense of pride or self-deprecation and references the fleeting nature of happiness.

  • 奈何欣然以为得其情也？ - Rhetorical question questioning the officials' judgment.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 嗟薄祐兮多幸 - A lament expressing a sense of being blessed despite one's lack of merit.
  • 羌作麗兮皇家 - Interjection expressing a sudden realization or emotion, meaning 'to be beautiful' or 'to adorn,' referring to being favored by the royal family.
  • 承昊穹兮下覆 - To receive the protection of the heavens, and 'to be covered or protected from below,' emphasizing dual protection.
  • 近日月兮分华 - To be close to the sun and moon, and 'to share in their brilliance,' suggesting being favored by celestial bodies.
  • 托后钧兮凝位 - To rely on the weight of the throne, and 'to stabilize one's position,' indicating a secure and honored place in the imperial hierarchy.
  • 忽前星兮启耀 - Suddenly, the star in front, and 'to shine brightly,' suggesting a sudden rise to prominence.
  • 虽衅累兮黄床 - Even though there are faults or sins, and '黄床' refers to the imperial bed or position, indicating awareness of one's shortcomings.
  • 庶无罪兮宗庙 - Perhaps without sin, and '宗庙' refers to the ancestral temples, suggesting a hope to avoid dishonor to the family lineage.
  • 欲贯鱼兮上进 - To want to follow the fish, a metaphor for aspiring to rise in rank, and '上进' means 'to advance upward.'
  • 乘阳德兮天飞 - To ride the virtue of the sun, and '天飞' means 'to fly to heaven,' suggesting a desire to achieve great heights.
  • 岂祸生兮无朕 - How could disaster arise, and '无朕' means 'without a sign,' indicating surprise or disbelief at sudden misfortune.
  • 蒙秽恶兮宫闱 - To be covered in filth and evil, and '宫闱' refers to the inner palace, suggesting a sense of being defiled or disgraced.
  • 将剖心兮自陈 - To be about to open one's heart, and '自陈' means 'to explain oneself,' indicating a willingness to confess or explain one's situation.
  • 冀回照兮白日 - To hope for a return of light, and '白日' means 'the bright sun,' suggesting a desire for clarity or justice to be restored.
  • 宁庶女兮多惭 - A humble woman, and '多惭' means 'to feel much shame,' indicating a sense of inadequacy or embarrassment.
  • 遏飞霜兮下击 - To stop the flying frost, and '下击' means 'to strike downward,' suggesting a desire to halt or counteract a negative force.
  • 顾子女兮哀顿 - To look at one's children, and '哀顿' means 'to be deeply saddened,' indicating sorrow for one's family.
  • 对左右兮衰伤 - To face those around, and '衰伤' means 'to be deeply grieved,' suggesting a sense of loss or grief among attendants or companions.
  • 共西曜兮将坠 - To share the setting sun, and '将坠' means 'to be about to fall,' indicating a sense of decline or impending doom.
  • 忽吾去兮椒房 - Suddenly, I leave, and '椒房' refers to the imperial harem, suggesting a sudden departure from the imperial court.
  • 呼天地兮惨悴 - To call upon heaven and earth, and '惨悴' means 'to be in a state of distress,' indicating profound sorrow or despair.
  • 恨今古兮安极 - To lament the present and past, and '安极' means 'to have no end,' suggesting endless regret or frustration.
  • 知吾生兮必死 - To know that I live, and '必死' means 'to be destined to die,' indicating an acceptance of mortality.
  • 又焉爱兮旦夕 - And why should I love, and '旦夕' means 'day and night,' suggesting a sense of futility or resignation in the face of impending death.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 昊穹 - Refers to the sky or heaven, often used in classical texts to denote the supreme power or the heavens.
  • 黄床 - Refers to the imperial bed or the imperial position, symbolizing the highest authority in the imperial court.
  • 宗庙 - Refers to the ancestral temples, important sites for rituals and ceremonies honoring ancestors.
  • 椒房 - Refers to the imperial harem, where the emperor's concubines and wives lived.
  • 西曜 - Refers to the setting sun, often used as a metaphor for the decline or end of something.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This opening line combines a lament and a realization, setting the tone for the entire text.
  • This line uses parallel structure to emphasize the dual protection and favor from both the heavens and the celestial bodies.
  • This line contrasts the stability of the imperial position with a sudden rise to prominence.
  • This line expresses a sense of guilt and a hope to avoid dishonor to the family lineage.
  • This line uses metaphors to express the desire for advancement and success.
  • This line expresses surprise and a sense of being defiled or disgraced.
  • This line expresses a willingness to confess and a hope for clarity or justice.
  • This line expresses a sense of inadequacy and a desire to counteract a negative force.
  • This line expresses sorrow for one's family and a sense of loss among one's attendants or companions.
  • This line expresses a sense of decline and a sudden departure from the imperial court.
  • This line expresses a profound sense of sorrow and endless regret.
  • This line expresses an acceptance of mortality and a sense of futility or resignation.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 惜多才，怜薄命 - Expresses sorrow for the talent of the deceased and pity for their short life.
  • 无计可留汝 - Indicates that there is no way to keep the deceased, emphasizing the inevitability of death.
  • 揉碎花笺，忍写断肠句 - Metaphorically describes the act of writing a poignant letter, with '揉碎花笺' (crumpling the flowered paper) and '忍写断肠句' (writing heart-wrenching words) conveying deep emotional pain.
  • 道傍杨柳依依 - A classical allusion to the *Book of Songs* (Shijing), where '杨柳依依' (willows swaying gently) symbolizes parting and lingering affection.
  • 千丝万缕，抵不住一分愁绪 - Metaphorically compares the myriad threads of sorrow to the gentle swaying of willows, indicating that even the most delicate emotions cannot be contained.
  • 捉月盟言，不是梦中语 - Refers to a famous legend of the Tang poet Li Bai, who drowned while trying to catch the moon's reflection in the water. '捉月盟言' (oath to catch the moon) and '不是梦中语' (not words in a dream) suggest that the promise made was real and not a mere fantasy.
  • 后回君若重来，不相忘处，把杯酒浇奴坟土 - A vow to remember the deceased and pour a cup of wine on their grave if they return, expressing a deep and lasting commitment.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 戴之 - The name of the deceased.

  • 牛奇章之子蔚 - The son of Niu Qizhang, who offered to help Deng Chang.
  • 李评事 - The name of the person Deng Chang was previously married to.
  • 牛氏 - The name of the woman Deng Chang later married.
  • 李氏 - The name of Deng Chang's first wife, Rui Shi.

  • 此妇性气，亦自可畏 - Describes the deceased's personality as formidable, indicating that she was strong-willed and possibly intimidating.
  • 昔邓敞以孤寒不第，牛奇章之子蔚谓敞曰 - Refers to a historical anecdote involving Deng Chang, a poor scholar who failed to pass the imperial examinations, and Niu Qizhang's son, who offered to help him marry his sister.
  • 吾父为宰相，兄弟皆在郎省 - Indicates that Niu Qizhang was a high-ranking official, and his sons held important positions in the government.
  • 纵不得富贵，岂无一嫁处邪 - Suggests that even if one does not achieve wealth or status, there are still opportunities for marriage.

  • 道傍杨柳依依，千丝万缕，抵不住一分愁绪 - The sequence of images builds up to the central theme of overwhelming sorrow.
  • 捉月盟言，不是梦中语 - The contrast between the reality of the oath and the dream-like quality of the promise adds depth to the emotional content.
  • 后回君若重来，不相忘处，把杯酒浇奴坟土 - The conditional structure and the act of pouring wine on the grave create a powerful image of remembrance and commitment.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 熙宁末 - End of the Xining era, a period in the reign of Emperor Shenzong of Song (1068-1085).
  • 洛中 - Means 'in Luoyang,' referring to the city of Luoyang during the Song Dynasty.
  • 耕于凤凰山下 - Literally means 'plowing under the Phoenix Mountain,' indicating the location where the discovery was made.
  • 获石碣 - Means 'found a stone stele,' referring to the discovery of an inscribed stone monument.
  • 方广一尺余 - Describes the size of the stone stele as being more than one chi (a traditional Chinese unit of length) square.
  • 乃妇人撰夫志铭 - Means 'then a woman wrote her husband's epitaph,' indicating the authorship and subject of the inscription.
  • 君姓曹氏，名禋，字礼夫 - Provides the personal details of the deceased, including his surname (曹), given name (禋), and courtesy name (礼夫).
  • 世为洛阳人 - Indicates that the deceased's family had been residents of Luoyang for generations.
  • 三十岁，两举不第，卒于长安道中 - Describes the deceased's age, his failure in two examinations, and his death on a road in Chang'an (the capital city during the Tang Dynasty).
  • 朝廷卿大夫、乡里故老闻之，莫不哀其‘孝友睦姻，笃行能文，何其夭之如是邪！’ - Expresses the widespread sorrow and lamentation over the deceased's early death, highlighting his virtues.
  • 唯儿闻之独不然，乃慰其母曰 - Means 'only the son heard it and did not react the same way, instead comforting his mother.'
  • 家有南亩，足以养其亲；室有遗文，足以教其子 - Indicates the son's reassurance to his mother about the family's resources and his father's legacy.
  • 凡累乎阴阳之间者，至死数不可逃，夫何悲喜之有哉 - Philosophical reflection on the inevitability of fate and the futility of emotional attachment to life and death.
  • 丙子年三月十八日卒，以其年十月十五日葬于凤凰山之原 - Provides the exact dates of the deceased's death and burial.
  • 余姓周氏，君妻也，归曹已八载矣 - Introduces the author of the epitaph, a woman surnamed Zhou, who had been married to the deceased for eight years.
  • 生子一人，尚幼 - Indicates that the deceased left behind one young son.
  • 以其恩义之不可忘，故作铭 - Explains the reason for writing the epitaph, emphasizing the importance of remembering the deceased's virtues.
  • 铭曰 - Introduces the inscription, which follows in the form of a poem.
  • 其生也天，其死也天，苟达此理，哀哉何言 - Reflects on the idea that both life and death are determined by fate, and thus, mourning is unnecessary.
  • 其生也浮，其死也休，终何为哉，慰母之忧 - Concludes the inscription by suggesting that the deceased's life was transient, and his death brought peace, ultimately serving to comfort his mother's grief.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 熙宁 - Refers to the Xining era, a significant period in the Song Dynasty marked by political and social reforms.
  • 洛中 - Refers to Luoyang, a historically important city in China, known for its cultural and political significance.
  • 凤凰山 - A specific mountain in Luoyang, where the stone stele was found.
  • 长安道 - Refers to a road in Chang'an, the capital city during the Tang Dynasty, indicating the place of the deceased's death.
  • 孝友睦姻 - Refers to the traditional Confucian virtues of filial piety, kindness to siblings, harmony in marriage, and good relationships with in-laws.
  • 笃行能文 - Describes the deceased's qualities of diligence and literary ability.
  • 丙子年 - Refers to the year 1076, using the Chinese sexagenary cycle.
  • 归曹 - Means 'married into the Cao family,' indicating the author's marriage to the deceased.
  • 南亩 - Refers to cultivated land, often used metaphorically to indicate the family's means of support.

  • 终教子建赋游龙 - Refers to Cao Zhi, a famous poet, and his work 'Roaming Dragon,' which could be a metaphor for poetic expression or a specific poem.
  • 绿珠碧玉心中事 - Refers to two historical figures, Liu Ling and Xi Shi, who are often used in literature to represent beauty and tragedy.
  • 今日谁知也到侬 - Who today knows that this has come to me.
  • 生得诗，知其以死许之，无复致望 - He received a poem, knowing that she had pledged her life to him, and he no longer had any hope.
  • 但愈加抑郁，遂感成疾 - But he became increasingly depressed and eventually fell ill.
  • 翠翠闻之，请于将军，始得一至，床前问候 - Cui Cui heard about it and asked the general, who finally allowed her to visit and greet him at his bedside.
  • 而生病已亟矣 - But his illness was already critical.
  • 翠翠以臂扶生而起，生引首侧视，凝泪满眶，长吁一声，奄然死于其手 - Cui Cui helps the man sit up, he looks sideways, tears well up in his eyes, he takes a deep breath, and then dies in her hands.
  • 将军怜之，葬于道场山麓 - The general pitied him and buried him at the foot of Daochang Mountain.
  • 翠翠送殡而归，是夜得疾，不复饮药，展转衾席，将及一月 - Cui Cui returned home after the funeral, and that night she fell ill, refusing to take medicine, tossing and turning in bed for nearly a month.
  • 一旦，告将军曰：“妾弃家相从，已得八载，流离外郡，举眼无亲，止有一兄，今又死矣！病必不能起，乞埋骨兄侧，使黄泉之下，庶有依托，不至作他乡孤鬼也！”言尽而卒 - One day, she told the general, 'I have left my family to follow him for eight years, wandering in distant lands, with no relatives around, only one brother, who is now dead! I know I will not recover from this illness, so I ask to be buried beside my brother, so that in the afterlife, I might have some support and not become a lonely ghost in a foreign land!' She said all this and then died.
  • 将军不违其志，竟附葬于生坟左，宛然东西二丘焉 - The general honored her wish and buried her beside his brother's grave, creating two adjacent mounds.
  • 事载瞿宗吉《剪灯新话》 - The story is recorded in Qu Zongji's 'New Tales of the Lamp.'
  • 后尚有翠翠家旧仆，以商贩过道场山，遇翠翠夫妇，寄书于父母。父买舟来访，徒见二坟，夜复梦翠翠云云 - Later, there was still an old servant from Cui Cui's family who, while traveling as a merchant, passed by Daochang Mountain and encountered the spirits of Cui Cui and her husband, who sent a letter to their parents. Her father bought a boat to visit, but saw only two graves, and at night he dreamed of Cui Cui.
  • 似涉小说家套数，今删之 - It seems to involve elements typical of fictional narratives, so it is omitted here.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 王琼奴 - A proper name, likely meaning 'Wang Qionnv,' where *琼奴* may be a poetic or honorific name, possibly meaning 'jade slave' or 'jade maiden,' though the exact meaning may be poetic or symbolic.
  • 二岁而父殁 - Classical narrative pattern: *age + event*.
  • 适 - Classical term for marriage, often used in the context of a woman being married off.
  • 爱有之过己生 - Compound structure: *爱有之* (loved her as if she were his own) + *过己生* (more than his own child).
  • 雅善 - Classical expression meaning 'excelled in' or 'was highly skilled in.'
  • 言、德、工、容四者咸备 - Classical reference to the four virtues of a woman (*言* speech, *德* virtue, *工* skill, *容* appearance).
  • 纳聘 - Traditional practice of offering a marriage gift or dowry.
  • 犹（尤）切 - *犹* is likely a typo or variant of *尤*, meaning 'especially' or 'particularly.'
  • 子 - Respectful term for 'son' or 'offspring.'
  • 华胄 - Refers to noble blood.
  • 白屋 - A humble or thatched house.
  • 犹豫迟疑，莫之能定 - Classical expression of indecision.
  • 有识者 - A person of insight or wisdom.
  • 择婿 - Classical term for selecting a husband.
  • 治具 - Making preparations.
  • 面试之 - Classical expression for an oral examination or interview.
  • 张筵 - Classical term for hosting a banquet.
  • 登降揖让 - Formal movements of bowing and greeting.
  • 衣冠 - Clothing and appearance.
  • 号 - Classical term for 'known as' or 'renowned as.'
  • 默识 - To recognize without speaking.
  • 咏之 - Classical term for composing or reciting poetry.
  • 睢盱 - Archaic term for looking around with a frown or a surly expression.
  • 染翰 - Classical term for writing or composing poetry with a brush.
  • 啧啧 - Onomatopoeic expression for repeated praise.
  • 云 - Classical particle used to introduce a quoted passage.

  • 王琼奴 - A historical or literary figure; no direct historical record, likely a fictional or semi-fictional character from a classical or vernacular Chinese story.
  • 童氏 - A surname, likely indicating the mother's family.
  • 沈必贵 - A wealthy man, possibly a merchant or landowner. The name is not historically known.
  • 徐从道、刘均玉 - Names of two men who are potential suitors. *从道* may imply a person who follows the path of virtue or learning; *均玉* may be a name with poetic or symbolic meaning.
  • 四画 - Four paintings: *《惜花春起早》, 《爱月夜眠迟》, 《掬水月在手》, 《弄花香满衣》*. These are classical poetic titles or images used in a literary or artistic context.
  • 耕云 - A name, possibly a pseudonym or nickname. The term *耕云* (plowing clouds) is a poetic expression, possibly indicating a person of leisure or intellectual pursuits.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 弄花香满衣 - Poetic title, likely meaning 'Playing with Flowers, Their Fragrance Fills the Robe.' Metaphor for sensuous and romantic atmosphere.
  • 铃声响处东风急 - Poetic way of indicating setting and mood, with the bell signaling a change in the environment.
  • 红紫丛边久凝立 - Standing long by the cluster of red and purple flowers. Description of a person lingering at a flower garden, emphasizing beauty and emotional response.
  • 素手攀条恐刺伤 - White hands reach out to grasp the branch, fearing the thorns may wound. Poetic image of delicate action with caution.
  • 金莲移步嫌苔湿 - Golden lotus steps move cautiously, disliking the damp moss. Refers to traditional Chinese practice of binding feet, showing elegance and sensitivity.
  • 幽芳撷罢掩兰堂 - After picking the fragrant flower, she closes the fragrant hall. Poetic expression of gathering flowers and retreating into private space.
  • 馥郁余馨满绣房 - The rich fragrance lingers in the embroidered chamber. Describes lingering scent of flowers in a private, luxurious room.
  • 蜂蝶纷纷入窗户 - Bees and butterflies flutter into the window. Metaphor for attraction of beauty or presence of romantic/sensual elements.
  • 飞来飞去绕罗裳 - Flying back and forth around the silk robe. Describes movement of bees and butterflies around the character’s clothing, symbolizing attraction and presence.
  • 辞莫措 - Unable to find words to respond. Indicates state of shock or embarrassment.
  • 大以为耻 - Consider it a great shame. Expresses strong disapproval or humiliation.
  • 不终席而逸矣 - Did not finish the meal and fled. Indicates abrupt departure, likely due to embarrassment or anger.
  • 合词称美 - All present composed a poem to praise. Indicates collective poetic praise, likely traditional literary practice.
  • 婚议遂成 - The marriage proposal was thus decided. Indicates conclusion of marriage arrangement.
  • 不出月，择日过聘 - Within the month, a date was chosen for the engagement. Formal statement about timeline of engagement.
  • 贵以爱婿故 - Valued because of his beloved son-in-law. Indicates reason for father’s favor.
  • 欲其数相往还 - Wanted him to visit frequently. Indicates desire for close interaction.
  • 遂招置馆中，读书游伴（泮） - Thus, he was invited to reside in the house, to study and be a companion (in the school). Term '泮' may refer to *泮宫* or Confucian school.
  • 小恙 - A minor illness. Polite term for slight ailment.
  • 侍汤药 - Served soup and medicine. Indicates care-giving.

  • 私喜 - Secretly rejoiced. Indicates personal, hidden feeling of pleasure.
  • 封红笺一幅 - Sealed a red letter. Traditional Chinese love letter, often associated with romantic or poetic communication.
  • 空纸也 - It was blank paper. Indicates absence of content, which may be metaphor or literal blank.
  • 笑成一绝 - Laughed and composed a quatrain. Indicates spontaneous poetic response.
  • 茜色霞笺照面频 - The crimson霞笺 shines on my face often. Poetic description of letter and its effect.
  • 玉郎何事太多情 - Why is the noble young man so full of affection? Question expressing surprise or teasing.
  • 风流不是无佳句 - Romance is not without beautiful verses. Indicates poetic expressions of love.
  • 两字相思写不成 - The two characters 'yearning' cannot be written. Poetic metaphor for difficulty of expressing deep emotion.
  • 持归，以夸于汉老 - Took it back and boasted to Han Lao. Indicates showing off or pride.
  • 方恨其夺己配也 - Only now did he resent that he had lost his match. Indicates jealousy or regret.
  • 诬以阴重事 - Accused them of serious hidden crimes. Indicates false accusations.

  • 自此南北各不相闻 - From then on, they heard nothing from each other. Indicates permanent separation or silence.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 已而必贵谢世 - A phrase suggesting a person will eventually attain high status but then pass away. '已而' means 'afterward' or 'eventually,' '必贵' means 'will surely become noble or wealthy,' and '谢世' means 'to pass away.'
  • 家事零落 - Describes the state of a family's affairs being in decline or scattered. '零落' means 'scattered' or 'in disarray.'

  • 吴知其故 - Wu knew the reason. '吴' means 'Wu,' '知' means 'knew,' and '其故' means 'the reason.'

  • 童氏惧 - The mother of Qiong Nu was afraid. '童氏' means 'Madam Tong,' and '惧' means 'afraid.'
  • 与琼谋曰 - Spoke with Qiong Nu saying. '与' means 'with,' '琼' means 'Qiong,' '谋曰' means 'spoke saying.'
  • 苕去五载，音问杳然 - Xu Lang has been gone for five years, and there is no news from him. '苕' means 'Xu Lang,' '去' means 'gone,' '五载' means 'five years,' '音问' means 'news,' and '杳然' means 'no news.'

  • 漫有玉京离燕 - Only have the swallows of Yujing that have left. '漫有' means 'only have,' '玉京' means 'Yujing,' '离燕' means 'swallows that have left.'
  • 向东风，似诉悲哀 - Facing the east wind, they seem to lament. '向' means 'facing,' '东风' means 'east wind,' '似' means 'seem,' and '诉悲哀' means 'lament.'

  • 不逢柳毅 - Did not meet Liu Yi. '不逢' means 'did not meet,' and '柳毅' means 'Liu Yi.'
  • 书 - The Book of Documents. '书' means 'the Book of Documents.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 已而必贵谢世 - This phrase reflects the traditional Chinese belief in the cycle of fortune and misfortune, where wealth and status are often temporary.
  • 家事零落 - This term is often used in classical Chinese literature to describe the decline of a family's fortunes.
  • 萧然茅店 - A thatched inn is a common setting in classical Chinese literature, often used to depict a humble or impoverished lifestyle.
  • 卖酒路旁 - Selling wine by the roadside is a common occupation for those in poverty, reflecting the economic hardships faced by many.
  • 虽患难之中 - This phrase emphasizes the resilience of individuals even in difficult circumstances.
  • 琼奴无从昔时容态 - This describes a change in appearance, often used to highlight the passage of time and the effects of hardship.
  • 青年粉质，终异常人 - Despite her current state, Qiong Nu still retains a youthful and extraordinary beauty, which is a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
  • 有吴指挥者悦之 - Wu Commander is a military official, and his attraction to Qiong Nu reflects the social dynamics of the time.
  • 欲娶为妾 - The practice of polygamy, particularly the taking of concubines, was common among the elite in ancient China.
  • 童氏以既聘辞 - The refusal based on the fact that Qiong Nu was already betrothed reflects the social norms and expectations of the time.

[656]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 信难裁 - Ambiguous phrase; could mean 'it is difficult to make a decision' or 'it is hard to control' depending on context.
  • 金钗脱股，宝镜离台 - Metaphors for separation and loss; '金钗脱股' refers to a golden hairpin falling from its holder, and '宝镜离台' refers to a precious mirror being removed from its stand, both symbolizing the separation of lovers.
  • 万里辽阳 - Metaphor for a long distance, often used to describe the separation of lovers.
  • 郎去也，甚日重回 - '郎' is a term of endearment for a man, often used by a woman to address her lover. '甚日重回' means 'when will you return?' expressing longing and uncertainty.
  • 丁香树，含花到死，肯傍别人开 - Metaphorical statement; '丁香树' (lilac tree) symbolizes a woman who remains faithful to her lover. '含花到死' means 'holding its flowers until death,' and '肯傍别人开' means 'would it bloom for someone else?' The whole phrase expresses unwavering loyalty and fidelity.
  • 自缢于房中 - Means 'hanged herself in the room,' indicating a tragic act of suicide.
  • 母觉而救解 - Means 'the mother noticed and saved her,' showing the intervention of the mother to prevent the suicide.
  • 吴指挥者闻之怒 - Means 'the commander Wu heard of it and was angry,' indicating the reaction of a high-ranking official.
  • 使麾下碎其酿器，逐去他居 - Means 'ordered his subordinates to smash her brewing equipment and drive her away to another place,' showing the harsh punishment meted out to the woman.
  • 欲折困之 - Means 'intended to break her spirit,' indicating the purpose behind the punishment.
  • 时有老驿使杜君 - Means 'there was an old postmaster named Du,' introducing a character who plays a significant role in the story.
  • 亦常山人 - Means 'also from Changshan,' providing additional information about the character.
  • 必贵存日，相与善 - Means 'when Bìguì was alive, they were good friends,' indicating a past relationship between the characters.
  • 怜童氏孤苦 - Means 'pitied Tóng's loneliness and hardship,' showing the character's compassion.
  • 假以驿廊一间而安焉 - Means 'gave her a room in the post station to stay,' indicating the character's kindness.
  • 一日，客有戎服者三四人投驿中 - Means 'one day, a few guests dressed in military uniforms arrived at the post station,' introducing new characters and setting the stage for the next part of the story.
  • 杜君问所从来，其人曰 - Means 'Du asked where they came from, and the people replied,' indicating the interaction between the characters.
  • 吾侪辽东某卫总小旗，差往海南取军，此假宿耳 - Means 'We are from a certain guard unit in Liaodong, sent to Hainan to recruit soldiers, and we are staying here for the night,' providing background information about the guests.
  • 值童氏偶出帘下 - Means 'Tóng happened to come out from behind the curtain,' describing a chance encounter.
  • 中一少年，特淳谨，不类武卒 - Means 'one of the young men was especially sincere and cautious, unlike a typical soldier,' highlighting the character's unusual demeanor.
  • 数往还相视，而凄惨之色可掬 - Means 'they looked at each other several times, and the sad expression was evident,' showing the emotional connection between the characters.
  • 童氏心动，因出问之 - Means 'Tóng was moved and went out to ask him,' indicating her curiosity and emotional response.
  • 对曰 - Means 'he replied,' starting the dialogue.
  • 苕姓徐，浙江常山人 - Means 'My name is Xu Tiáo, from Changshan, Zhejiang,' providing the character's name and origin.
  • 幼时，父尝聘同里沈必贵女 - Means 'When I was young, my father once betrothed me to a girl from the same village, Shěn Bìguì's daughter,' providing background information about the character's past.
  • 未婚，而两家坐事谪戍，不相闻者数年矣 - Means 'We were not married, but both families were exiled to military service, and we lost contact for several years,' explaining the separation.
  • 适因入室，见妈妈状貌，酷与苕外母相类，故不觉感怆，非有他也 - Means 'Just now, when I entered the room, I saw your mother's appearance, which was very similar to my late mother-in-law, so I couldn't help but feel sad, it's nothing else,' explaining the emotional reaction.
  • 童氏复问 - Means 'Tóng asked again,' continuing the conversation.
  • 沈家今在何处？厥女何名 - Means 'Where is Shěn's family now? What is the name of his daughter?' asking for more information.
  • 曰 - Means 'he replied,' continuing the dialogue.
  • 女名琼奴，字润贞 - Means 'Her name is Qióngnú, and her courtesy name is Rùnzhen,' providing the name of the girl.
  • 联亲时年方十四，以今计之，当十九矣 - Means 'When we were betrothed, she was fourteen years old. By now, she should be nineteen,' providing the age of the girl.
  • 第知戍海南，忘其所寓州郡，难已寻觅 - Means 'But I only know that she is stationed in Hainan, and I have forgotten the state or prefecture where she lives, making it difficult to find her,' expressing the difficulty in locating her.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 海南 - A province in southern China, historically known for its strategic importance and military recruitment.

  • 驿使 - A postmaster, a position responsible for managing post stations along the postal routes.

  • 戎服 - Military uniform, indicating the characters' military status.
  • 小旗 - A low-ranking military officer, indicating the rank of the guests.
  • 谪戍 - Exile to military service, a common punishment in ancient China.
  • 琼奴，字润贞 - Names and courtesy names, indicating the cultural practice of giving both a given name and a courtesy name.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 召至室中细问之 - Called to the room and asked in detail.

  • 杜君乃率钱备礼，与苕毕姻 - Mr. Du led the way in preparing money and gifts, married Tao.

  • 吾将择日，舁轿相迎 - I will choose a date, carry a sedan chair to welcome you.

  • 母亦无如之何 - Her mother could do nothing about it.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 京師人王武功 - Standard classical biographical structure: [place of origin] + [name].
  • 居韈（亻幼）巷 - Lived in the 'Shu' (韈) Lane. The character 韈 is unusual here. It may be a phonetic or variant form of 亻幼 (a person with a child), but this is uncertain. Alternatively, it may be a misspelled or archaic place name. The character in parentheses (亻幼) may be a suggested correction or variant. This is a potential error in the original text.
  • 化缘僧 - A 'begging monk' or 'monk who collects alms.' This is a common term for monks in the context of begging or traveling for alms.
  • 阴设挑致之策 - 'Secretly devised a plan to seduce her.' '阴设' means to secretly plan, and '挑致' is to entice or seduce.
  • 未得便 - 'Had not yet found an opportunity.' This is a common classical expression for being unable to act due to lack of opportunity.
  • 会王生将赴官淮上 - 'Coincidentally, Wang was about to take office in Huai.' '会' here means 'happened to coincide with' or 'just as.'
  • 与妻坐帘内 - 'Sat with his wife behind the curtain.' This suggests a private setting, possibly within a home.
  • 一外仆顶盒至前 - 'A servant came forward with a box on his head.' This is a formal way of describing a servant bringing an object, possibly in a ceremonial or respectful manner.
  • 云：“聪大师传信县君，相别有日，无以表意，漫奉此送路。” - 'Said: 'Master Cong is sending this message to Lady County, as we will be separated for some time. I have nothing to offer in return, so I humbly present this to send you on your way.' This is a polite and formal way of delivering a message, with '漫奉此送路' meaning 'humbly present this as a farewell gift.'
  • 语讫即去 - 'After speaking, he left.' This is a standard classical narrative structure.
  • 王夫妇亟启盒 - 'Wang and his wife hurriedly opened the box.' '亟' means 'hurriedly' or 'immediately.'
  • 乃肉茧百枚 - 'Inside were a hundred meat pods.' '肉茧' is a rare or unusual term; it may refer to something resembling a pod or husk, possibly containing something hidden inside.
  • 剖其中，藏小金牌饼，重一钱 - 'When they cut it open, they found a small golden ingot, weighing one qian.' '金牌饼' is a term that could refer to a small gold coin or ingot shaped like a cake. '重一钱' specifies the weight in qian (an ancient Chinese unit of weight).
  • 以为误也，复剖其他尽然 - 'Thinking it was a mistake, they cut open the rest, and they were all the same.' This shows that the couple was surprised and checked all the pods.
  • 王作声叱妻曰 - 'Wang shouted at his wife, saying...' This is a dramatic way of showing anger or accusation.
  • 我疑此秃朝夕往来于门，必有故，今果尔 - 'I suspected this monk was coming to our door every day for some reason, and now it has come to light.' This is a direct accusation.
  • 即诉于县府 - 'He immediately reported it to the county office.' This is a standard legal procedure.
  • 僧已窜，不知名字、居止，无从缉捕 - 'The monk had already fled; his name and residence were unknown, so there was no way to arrest him.' This is a common narrative in classical Chinese legal cases.
  • 王弃妻单车赴任 - 'Wang abandoned his wife and rode alone to take up his post.' This is a strong statement of betrayal and emotional distance.
  • 妻亦无以自明 - 'His wife had no way to prove her innocence.' This is a common expression of helplessness.
  • 囚系累月，府尹以为疑狱 - 'She was imprisoned for many months, and the county magistrate considered it a case of doubt.' This shows the legal uncertainty.
  • 命录付外舍 - 'Ordered her to be sent to an external dormitory.' This may refer to a place of detention or confinement.
  • 穷无取食 - 'She was poor and had no food to eat.' This is a direct statement of hardship.
  • 僧闻而潜归 - 'The monk heard and secretly returned.' This is a dramatic turn in the narrative.
  • 密赂针妇，说之曰 - 'He secretly bribed a seamstress and said to her...' This is a common narrative device in classical Chinese fiction.
  • 汝今日饿死矣，我引尔至某寺，为大众僧缝纫度日，以俟武功回心何如？ - 'You will die of starvation today. I will take you to a certain temple and have you sew for the monks to live, waiting for Wang to change his mind. How does that sound?' This is a persuasive and manipulative speech.
  • 王妻勉从其言 - 'Wang’s wife reluctantly followed his words.' This shows her desperation.
  • 既往，正入前僧之室，藏于地穽 - 'After going there, she entered the room of the previous monk and was hidden in a pit.' '地穽' likely refers to a hidden pit or trap, possibly a secret chamber.
  • 奸侮自如 - 'He raped her at his leisure.' This is a blunt and direct expression of violence.
  • 久而稍听其出入 - 'After some time, she began to hear his comings and goings.' This shows her growing awareness.
  • 遂伺隙告逻卒 - 'She then waited for an opportunity to inform the patrol guards.' This is a common narrative of escape or revenge.
  • 执僧到官，伏罪 - 'The guards captured the monk and brought him to the authorities, who made him confess.' This is a standard legal conclusion.
  • 王妻亦怀恨以死 - 'Wang’s wife also died, filled with hatred.' This is a tragic ending, emphasizing her emotional suffering.
