  • 高文遥 - Gao Wenyao, an official.
  • 昙献 - Tanxian, a Buddhist monk.
  • 胡床 - A type of chair or seat, possibly foreign in origin.
  • 武成 - Emperor Wu Cheng of the Northern Qi.
  • 昭立统 - Uncertain, likely a religious or monastic title.

[711]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 僧徒遥指太后，以弄昙献 - Monks pointed to the empress from afar, toying with Tanxian. '遥指' (pointing from afar) and '弄' (toying with) describe a manipulative action.
  • 太上者 - The Supreme One, a title often given to emperors or high-ranking officials.
  • 帝闻太后不谨，而未之信 - The emperor heard that the empress was not chaste, but did not believe it. '不谨' (not chaste) is a euphemism for infidelity.
  • 二少尼 - Two young nuns, where '少尼' refers to young female Buddhist monks.
  • 悦而召之，乃男子也 - He was pleased and summoned them, only to find they were men. '悦' (pleased) and '召之' (summoned them) are straightforward, but the revelation that they were men adds a twist.
  • 伏法 - To be punished by law, a euphemism for execution.
  • 元山王三郡君，皆太后所昵也 - The Prince of Yuanshan, the Three Marquisates, and the Three Counties, all close to the empress. '所昵' (close to) indicates a personal relationship.
  • 帝视晋阳，奉太后还邺 - The emperor looked to Jinyang and escorted the empress back to Ye. '视' (looked to) and '奉' (escorted) are formal terms.
  • 紫陌 - Purple Road, a poetic name for a road, often associated with imperial travel.
  • 舍人魏僧伽明风角 - The attendant Wei Sengga was skilled in divination. '风角' (divination) refers to a specific form of fortune-telling.
  • 即时当有暴逆事 - There will be a sudden and violent uprising. '暴逆' (violent uprising) is a serious accusation.
  • 弯弓缠矟驰入城 - He drew his bow, wrapped his spear, and galloped into the city. '弯弓' (drew his bow) and '缠矟' (wrapped his spear) are vivid descriptions of preparation for action.
  • 幽太后北宫 - Imprisoned the empress in the Northern Palace. '幽' (imprisoned) is a strong term.
  • 敕内外诸亲，一不得与太后相见 - Ordered all relatives inside and outside the palace not to see the empress. '敕' (ordered) is a formal command.
  • 周使元伟来聘 - The Zhou envoy Yuan Wei came to pay a visit. '聘' (visit) can also mean to pay respects.
  • 作《述行赋》 - Wrote the 'Fu on the Journey'. '述行' (journey) and '赋' (fu) are literary terms.
  • 郑庄公克段而迁姜氏 - Duke Zhuang of Zheng defeated his brother Duan and exiled Jiangshi. This is a reference to a historical event from the *Zuo Zhuan*.
  • 文虽不工，当时深以为愧 - Although the writing was not refined, it deeply shamed the contemporaries. '不工' (not refined) and '深以为愧' (deeply ashamed) are evaluative phrases.
  • 齐亡入周，恣行奸秽 - After the fall of Qi, he entered Zhou and acted shamelessly. '恣行' (acted shamelessly) and '奸秽' (shameful acts) are strong terms.
  • 隋开皇中殂 - He died in the Kaihuang era of the Sui dynasty. '殂' (died) is a formal term.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 僧徒 - Monks, referring to Buddhist monks.
  • 太后 - Empress, a high-ranking female figure in the imperial court.
  • 昙献 - A historical figure, possibly a monk or a court official.
  • 太上者 - A title often given to emperors or high-ranking officials.
  • 晋阳 - Jinyang, an ancient city in Shanxi province.
  • 邺 - Ye, an ancient capital of the Cao Wei kingdom.
  • 紫陌 - Purple Road, a poetic name for a road, often associated with imperial travel.
  • 舍人魏僧伽 - Attendant Wei Sengga, a historical figure known for his skills in divination.
  • 风角 - A form of divination.
  • 暴逆 - Violent uprising, a serious political threat.
  • 北宫 - Northern Palace, a specific palace in the imperial complex.
  • 周使元伟 - Zhou envoy Yuan Wei, a historical figure who visited the court.
  • 《述行赋》 - Fu on the Journey, a literary work.
  • 郑庄公克段而迁姜氏 - A historical event from the *Zuo Zhuan*.
  • 齐亡入周 - Refers to the fall of the Qi state and the subsequent political changes.
  • 隋开皇中 - The Kaihuang era of the Sui dynasty.

[712]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 逼通 - Euphemism for sexual coercion; indicates that Empress Hu forced herself upon Yang Baihua.
  • 追思 - To remember or grieve for the past; describes Empress Hu's ongoing grief.
  • 连臂蹋足 - Traditional dance where people hold hands and stomp their feet; describes the performance of the song by palace women.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 魏灵太后 - Empress Hu, a powerful figure in Northern Wei history, known for her political acumen and controversial policies.
  • 杨白花 - Historical figure whose story is often cited in Chinese literature and folklore; his flight to Liang and the subsequent song by Empress Hu are part of this narrative.
  • 梁 - The Southern Dynasty, a rival state to Northern Wei, known for its cultural and literary achievements.
  • 杨白花歌 - Song composed by Empress Hu in memory of Yang Baihua; a historical example of how literature and music were used to express personal and political sentiments in ancient China.

Proper Nouns

  • 魏灵太后 - Empress Hu, the regent during the reign of Emperor Xiaoming of Northern Wei.
  • 武都 - A region in modern-day Gansu and Sichuan, known for its historical significance.
  • 杨白花 - Yang Baihua, a historical figure whose story is often cited in Chinese literature and folklore.
  • 梁 - The Southern Dynasty, a rival state to Northern Wei.
  • 杨白花歌 - The song composed by Empress Hu in memory of Yang Baihua.

[713]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 得楊花淚沾臆 - Poetic expression meaning 'obtaining the tears of willow flowers that wet the chest'; metaphor for sorrow or emotional distress.
  • 秋去春來雙燕子 - Classical idiom meaning 'as seasons change from autumn to spring, the double swallows return'; reflects cyclical nature of time and return of love or companionship.
  • 宣武於洛陽立瑶光寺 - Xuanwu established the Ruyao Temple in Luoyang; formal statement about temple construction.
  • 為椒房學道之所 - As a place where the imperial concubine (椒房) studies Daoist teachings.
  • 掖庭美人 - Beauties of the Yeching Palace; refers to palace concubines or favored women in the imperial harem.
  • 亦有名族處女，來儀此寺 - Noble-born maidens from distinguished families came to this temple; *來儀* is a poetic term for visiting or arriving in a respectful or ritual manner.
  • 及爾朱兆入洛 - When Erzhuzhao entered Luoyang; refers to a historical event involving a military invasion or takeover.
  • 縱兵大掠 - Allowed his soldiers to pillage and loot; common expression for military plundering.
  • 時有秀容胡騎數十，入瑶光寺淫秽 - Dozens of Xiu Rong Hu (Tibetan or northern nomadic) horsemen entered Ruyao Temple and committed indecency; *淫秽* means indecent or lewd acts.
  • 自后颇获讥讪 - From then on, it was often mocked or ridiculed; *讥讪* means to mock, criticize, or scorn.
  • 京師語曰 - The capital city's saying went...; colloquial expression for a common saying or proverb in the capital.
  • 隋宣華夫人陳氏 - Chen Shi, the Xuanhua夫人 of the Sui dynasty; *夫人* is a title for a noblewoman or consort.
  • 陳宣帝之女也 - Daughter of Emperor Xuan of Chen; *宣帝* is the title of a historical emperor.
  • 性聰慧，姿貌無雙 - She was intelligent and had unmatched beauty; standard poetic description of a virtuous and attractive woman.
  • 及陳滅，配掖庭 - After the fall of Chen, she was assigned to the Yeching Palace; *配掖庭* refers to being assigned to the imperial harem or palace.
  • 后選入宮為嬪 - Later, she was selected to enter the palace as a concubine.
  • 獨孤皇后性妒 - The Empress Dougu was jealous; *獨孤* is a clan name, referring to Empress Dougu, wife of Emperor Wen of Sui.
  • 后宮罕得進御 - Rarely did the concubines gain access to the emperor; *進御* means to be summoned or favored by the emperor.
  • 惟陳氏有寵 - Only Chen Shi was favored; contrast highlighting her special status.
  • 晉王廣之在藩也 - When Prince Jin Guang was still a vassal prince; *晉王* is the title of a prince, later Emperor Yang of Sui.
  • 陰有奪宗之計 - Secretly plotted to seize the throne; *奪宗* means to usurp the imperial throne.
  • 視為內助 - Viewed her as an inner helper; *內助* is a term for a powerful or supportive woman in court.
  • 每致禮焉 - He always paid her respects; formal expression of respect or deference.
  • 進金蛇、金駝等物 - Presented golden snakes, golden camels, and other items; symbolic gifts, possibly meant to impress or bribe.
  • 以取媚於陳氏 - In order to flatter Chen Shi; *取媚* means to win favor or please.
  • 皇太子廢立之际 - At the time of the emperor's decision to depose and install a new crown prince; refers to a political upheaval.
  • 專戾擅寵 - She monopolized the emperor's favor and was domineering; *專戾* suggests tyranny or overbearing behavior.
  • 主斷內事 - She made the final decisions on internal matters; *主斷* means to take the final decision.
  • 六宮莫與為比 - No one in the six palaces could rival her; *六宮* refers to the six palaces or concubines of the imperial harem.
  • 及上大漸 - When the emperor was gravely ill; *大漸* is a classical term for a severe illness.
  • 遺詔拜為宣華夫人 - In his dying will, he posthumously conferred upon her the title of Xuanhua夫人; *遺詔* is a will or last testament.
  • 初，上寢疾於仁壽宮也 - At the beginning, when the emperor was ill in the Renshou Palace; *寢疾* is a euphemism for being seriously ill.
  • 平旦出更衣 - At dawn, she went to change her clothes; *平旦* is early morning; *更衣* is to change clothes.
  • 為太子所逼 - She was pressured by the crown prince; *逼* means to pressure, coerce, or force.
  • 夫人拒之得免 - The lady refused and thus escaped; *拒之* is to refuse; *得免* is to escape or avoid.
  • 歸於上所，怪其神色有異 - Returning to the emperor, she noticed something strange about his expression; *神色有異* means his expression was unusual or changed.
  • 問其故，夫人泫然曰 - She asked him why, and the lady wept and said...; *泫然* is to weep or look tearful.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 楊花淚 - Willow flower tears; in Chinese poetry, willow flowers often symbolize sorrow, parting, or unrequited love; *淚沾臆* suggests the emotional impact of this sorrow on the chest, a common metaphor for deep sadness.
  • 雙燕子 - Double swallows; well-known poetic image in Chinese literature, symbolizing love, reunion, or the return of a loved one; swallows are often associated with spring and the cyclical nature of life.
  • 宣武 - Xuanwu, likely referring to Emperor Xuanwu of Northern Wei (515–528 CE), a Northern Wei emperor known for his patronage of Buddhism and temple-building.
  • 瑶光寺 - Ruyao Temple, a Buddhist temple built in Luoyang; its name may be symbolic or derived from Daoist or Buddhist cosmology.
  • 椒房 - Term referring to the living quarters of the empress or concubine in the palace; often used metaphorically to describe the position of a favored concubine.
  • 掖庭 - Yeching Palace, the imperial harem where concubines and palace women were kept.
  • 爾朱兆 - Erzhuzhao, a general and warlord of the Northern Wei dynasty who led a rebellion and later became emperor; his invasion of Luoyang is historically significant.
  • 秀容胡騎 - Xiu Rong Hu cavalry; refers to the northern nomadic tribes, likely the Xiongnu or Tujue, who were often associated with invasions of the Chinese heartland.
  • 淫秽 - Indecent acts or lewd behavior; a term that would have been shocking in the context of a Buddhist temple.
  • 京師語曰 - The capital city's saying went...; reference to popular sayings or proverbs circulating in the imperial capital, often reflecting social or political sentiment.
  • 隋宣華夫人陳氏 - Chen Shi, the Xuanhua夫人 of the Sui dynasty; this is a historical figure, likely a concubine or consort of Emperor Wen of Sui.
  • 陳宣帝 - Emperor Xuan of Chen, the ruler of the Southern Dynasty Chen (557–589 CE), a contemporary of the Northern dynasties.
  • 獨孤皇后 - Empress Dougu, the wife of Emperor Wen of Sui, known for

[714]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 上恚曰 - The emperor was angry and said, where '恚' (hui) means anger or resentment.
  • 畜生何足付大事 - What kind of beast is fit for great matters? An expression of contempt, suggesting that the person being referred to is unworthy of important responsibilities.
  • 独孤诚误我 - Dugu Cheng has misled me, where '误我' (wu wo) means to mislead or deceive the speaker.
  • 召我儿 - Summon my son, a direct command to bring the emperor's son.
  • 勇也 - It's Yong, referring to the name of the emperor's son, Yang Yong.
  • 书讫 - After writing it down, indicating the completion of a written order.
  • 示左仆射杨素 - Show it to the Left Minister Yang Su, where '示' (shi) means to show or display, and '左仆射' (zuoyushu) is the title of a high-ranking official.
  • 太子遣张衡入寝殿 - The crown prince sent Zhang Heng into the sleeping chamber, where '遣' (qian) means to send, and '寝殿' (qindian) refers to the emperor's private quarters.
  • 遂令夫人及后宫同侍疾者，并出就别宫 - And ordered the empress and the consorts who were tending to the emperor's illness to leave and go to another palace, where '遂' (sui) means then or subsequently, and '别宫' (biegong) refers to a different palace.
  • 俄闻上崩 - Soon after, they heard that the emperor had died, where '俄' (e) means soon, and '崩' (beng) is the euphemism for the death of an emperor.
  • 夫人与诸后宫相顾曰 - The empress and the consorts looked at each other and said, where '相顾' (xianggu) means to look at each other.
  • 得免死矣 - We have been spared from death, expressing relief.
  • 陈氏恚而却坐 - Lady Chen was angry and sat back, where '恚' (hui) means anger, and '却坐' (quezuo) means to sit back or retreat.
  • 不肯致谢诣 - Refused to go and thank him, where '致谢' (zhixie) means to express gratitude, and '诣' (yi) means to go to or visit.
  • 其夜，太子烝焉 - That night, the crown prince had relations with her, where '烝' (zheng) is a euphemism for sexual intercourse, often used in historical texts to avoid explicit language.
  • 炀帝即位之后 - After Emperor Yang ascended the throne, where '炀帝' (yangdi) is the posthumous name of Emperor Yang of Sui.
  • 出居先都宫 - Was sent out to live in the former capital palace, where '先都宫' (xiandugong) refers to the old imperial palace.
  • 寻召入 - Later, he was summoned back, where '寻' (xun) means soon or shortly.
  • 岁余而终 - After more than a year, she died, where '岁余' (suoyu) means more than a year.
  • 帝深悼之，为制《伤神赋》 - The emperor deeply mourned her and composed the 'Grief of the Spirit' ode, where '悼之' (diao zhi) means to mourn, and '伤神赋' (shangshenfu) is the title of the ode.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 上 - Refers to the emperor, specifically Emperor Wen of Sui.
  • 独孤诚 - A high-ranking official, likely a trusted advisor to the emperor.
  • 太子 - Refers to the crown prince, Yang Yong, who later became Emperor Wen of Sui.
  • 炀帝 - The posthumous name of Emperor Yang of Sui, the second emperor of the Sui dynasty.
  • 伤神赋 - A literary work composed by Emperor Yang of Sui to mourn the death of Yang Yong's mother.

Proper Nouns

  • 上 - The emperor, Emperor Wen of Sui.
  • 独孤诚 - A high-ranking official, likely a trusted advisor to the emperor.
  • 太子 - The crown prince, Yang Yong.
  • 炀帝 - The posthumous name of Emperor Yang of Sui.
  • 伤神赋 - A literary work composed by Emperor Yang of Sui to mourn the death of Yang Yong's mother.

[715]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 得幸 - Euphemism meaning 'to gain favor' or 'to be favored by a ruler,' often used in contexts of sexual or political favor.
  • 為才人 - A title in the imperial court, meaning 'a woman of talent or skill,' often used to denote a concubine or female official.
  • 賜號武媚 - 'Bestowed the title of Wu Mei,' a nickname or honorific given to her by Emperor Taizong.
  • 入侍太宗疾 - 'Entered the service to attend to Emperor Taizong’s illness,' indicating her role as a caretaker or nurse.
  • 悅之，遂即東厢烝焉 - 'Found her pleasing, and then immediately had relations with her in the eastern chamber.' The term 烝 is a classical euphemism for incestuous or unlawful relations, likely meaning adultery or incest in this context, as she was a servant at the time.
  • 為尼 - 'Became a nun,' indicating her retreat from court life after the death of Emperor Taizong.
  • 忌日 - 'The day of mourning' or 'the anniversary of the death of a ruler or important figure.'
  • 行香 - 'To burn incense,' a ritual act of worship or veneration.
  • 見上而淚 - 'Saw the Emperor and wept,' indicating an emotional display.
  • 王后疾蕭淑妃之寵 - 'The Empress Wang resented the favor given to Xiao Shufei.'
  • 陰令武氏長發 - 'Secretly ordered Wu to let her hair grow long,' a traditional way of indicating a woman's readiness for marriage or entry into the imperial harem.
  • 納之後宮 - 'Admitted her into the palace,' meaning she was brought into the imperial harem.
  • 巧慧多權術 - 'Clever and wise, with many strategies,' indicating her political acumen.
  • 屈體事后 - 'Submitted herself humbly to the Empress,' showing deference and servitude.
  • 后數稱其美 - 'The Empress frequently praised her beauty,' indicating her growing favor.
  • 大幸 - 'Great favor,' referring to being chosen by the emperor for sexual relations.
  • 拜為昭儀 - 'Appointed as a Zhaoyi,' a high-ranking imperial concubine title.
  • 更相與譖之 - 'Turned against each other and accused her,' indicating rivalry and conspiracy.
  • 上皆不納 - 'The Emperor did not accept their accusations.'
  • 生子 - 'Gave birth to a son,' indicating the birth of a male heir.
  • 欲廢后而立之 - 'Wanted to depose the Empress and replace her with Wu.'
  • 褚遂良諫曰 - 'Chu Suiliang advised the Emperor, saying...'
  • 武氏在帘中大言曰 - 'Wu, behind the curtain, loudly said...' indicating her presence and boldness.
  • 何不扑殺此獠 - 'Why not strike down this villain?' a harsh and aggressive statement.
  • 逐遂良 - 'Exiled Chu Suiliang.'
  • 立武氏 - 'Established Wu as Empress.'
  • 王皇后與蕭妃並廢 - 'Empress Wang and Xiao Shufei were both deposed.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 唐高宗武后 - 'Tang Gaozong’s Wu Empress,' referring to Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
  • 太宗 - 'Emperor Taizong of Tang,' the second emperor of the Tang dynasty, known for his strong rule and military achievements.
  • 高宗 - 'Emperor Gaozong of Tang,' the third emperor, known for his long reign and his marriage to Wu Zetian.
  • 才人 - A title given to imperial concubines, indicating a position of relative status and talent.
  • 昭儀 - A high-ranking title among the imperial concubines, indicating favor and status.
  • 王后 - 'Empress Wang,' the first wife of Emperor Gaozong.
  • 蕭淑妃 - 'Xiao Shufei,' a concubine of Emperor Gaozong who was one of Wu Zetian’s main rivals.
  • 褚遂良 - A high-ranking official and Confucian scholar who opposed Wu Zetian’s rise to power.
  • 忌日 - A day of mourning for a deceased emperor or noble, often marked by religious rituals.
  • 行香 - A Buddhist or Daoist ritual involving the burning of incense as an offering.
  • 尼 - A Buddhist nun, indicating Wu Zetian’s retreat from the imperial court after Emperor Taizong’s death.
  • 烝 - A term often used in Classical Chinese to denote incestuous or unlawful relations, in this case likely referring to adultery between the emperor and a servant.
  • 廢后 - 'Depose the Empress,' a serious political act in imperial China, often leading to conflict and power struggles.
  • 并废 - 'Both were deposed,' indicating the simultaneous removal of two rival figures.

Proper Nouns

  • 唐高宗 - Emperor Gaozong of Tang.
  • 武后 - Wu Zetian, the later Empress Wu, the only female emperor of China.
  • 太宗 - Emperor Taizong of Tang.
  • 高宗 - Emperor Gaozong of Tang.
  • 武媚 - A nickname or honorific given to Wu Zetian by Emperor Taizong.
  • 王后 - Empress Wang, the first wife of Emperor Gaozong.
  • 萧淑妃 - Xiao Shufei, a rival concubine of Wu Zetian.
  • 褚遂良 - Chu Suiliang, a high-ranking official and Confucian scholar who opposed Wu Zetian’s rise to power.

[716]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 遂良不谏于蓄发纳之宫中之日 - Complex and elliptical phrase meaning Suizheng did not advise against keeping her hair and admitting her into the palace at that time, emphasizing a missed opportunity.
  • 而谏于宠深爱笃欲立为后之时 - Contrasts with the previous phrase, meaning Suizheng did advise against it when she was deeply loved and desired to be made empress.
  • 呜呼，晚矣 - Classical expression of lamentation, meaning 'Alas, it is too late!' or 'Ah, too late!'
  • 虽列位从官，而后内衔之 - Although they held official positions, Wu Zetian harbored resentment toward them.
  • 后置堇毒，杀魏国夫人，归罪惟良等，尽杀之 - Sequence of actions: Wu Zetian placed poison, killed Lady Weiguo, blamed Weiliang and others, and had them all killed.
  • 元庆、元爽从坐，流龙州、振州死 - Yuanqing and Yuanshuang were implicated, exiled to Longzhou and Zhenzhou, where they died.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 遂良 - Suizheng (褚遂良), a high-ranking official and key figure in the Tang court, known for opposing Wu Zetian’s rise to power.
  • 武氏 - Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history, who later became the Empress Wu of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 杨氏 - Lady Yang, the mother of Wu Zetian, who was originally a concubine of Emperor Taizong of Tang.
  • 贺兰氏 - The Helan family, a prominent aristocratic family in the Tang Dynasty, with whom Wu Zetian had ties.
  • 魏国夫人 - A title granted to Wu Zetian’s daughter, who was extremely favored by the emperor.
  • 相里二子元庆、元爽 - The two sons of Xiangli, a family whose members were closely associated with the imperial court.
  • 从兄惟良、怀运 - Wu Zetian’s cousins, Weiliang and Huaiyun, who were part of the imperial family but were later executed by her.
  • 封泰山 - A ritual of imperial sacrifice at Mount Tai, a significant political and religious ceremony in the Tang Dynasty.
  • 岳牧 - Officials in charge of the provinces or regions, who would attend the emperor at such ceremonies.
  • 堇毒 - "Jin" is a type of poison, likely referring to a toxic plant used in poisoning.
  • 士彟 - Shi Yuo, Wu Zetian’s first husband, a high-ranking official during Emperor Taizong’s reign.
  • 贺兰敏之 - Helan Minzhi, a member of the Helan family, who was adopted by Wu Zetian as the heir to her late husband Shi Yuo.
  • 袭封周国公 - To inherit the title of 'Zhuguo Gong' (Lord of Zhou), a hereditary noble title.

Proper Nouns

  • 遂良 - Proper noun referring to褚遂良 (Chǔ Suìliáng), a Tang Dynasty official.
  • 武氏 - Proper noun referring to Wu Zetian (武则天), the female emperor of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 杨氏 - Proper noun referring to Lady Yang (杨氏), the mother of Wu Zetian.
  • 贺兰氏 - Proper noun referring to the Helan family, an aristocratic clan.
  • 魏国夫人 - Proper noun referring to the title of Wu Zetian’s daughter.
  • 相里二子元庆、元爽 - Proper nouns referring to the two sons of Xiangli, a family name.
  • 惟良、怀运 - Proper nouns referring to Wu Zetian’s cousins.
  • 士彟 - Proper noun referring to Wu Zetian’s first husband.
  • 贺兰敏之 - Proper noun referring to the adopted heir of Wu Zetian.
  • 周国公 - Proper title referring to a hereditary noble rank.

[717]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 敏之少韶秀 - Describes Li Shimin as youthful, handsome, and charming.
  • 輕俊自喜 - Indicates that he was proud of his own elegance and grace.
  • 與私通 - Refers to an illicit relationship.
  • 俾繼士彟 - Means 'to make him the heir of Li Shiji' (Li Shiji was Li Shimin's father).
  • 逼淫之 - Indicates forced sexual intercourse.
  • 固辭 - Means to firmly refuse or decline.
  • 乾没自用 - Refers to embezzlement or misappropriation of funds.
  • 褫衰粗 - Means to remove mourning clothes.
  • 奏音樂 - Refers to playing music.
  • 悉逼亂之 - Indicates that he forced and corrupted all the palace attendants who came to visit his mother.
  • 流雷州 - Means to be exiled to Leizhou.
  • 表復故姓 - Refers to a petition to restore his original surname.
  • 道中自經死 - Means that he hanged himself along the way.
  • 奉士彟后 - Means to be adopted as the heir of Li Shiji.
  • 進號天后 - Means to be advanced to the title of Empress.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 敏之 - Refers to Li Shimin, a member of the Tang imperial family.
  • 楊氏 - Refers to Yang Guifei's grandmother, who had an illicit relationship with Li Shimin.
  • 士彟 - Refers to Li Shiji, the father of Li Shimin.
  • 太平公主 - Refers to Princess Taiping, a powerful figure in the Tang dynasty.
  • 上元元年 - Refers to the first year of the Shangyuan era (760 CE).

Proper Nouns

  • 敏之 - Li Shimin
  • 楊氏 - Yang Guifei's grandmother
  • 士彟 - Li Shiji
  • 后 - Wu Zetian (Empress Wu)
  • 太平公主 - Princess Taiping
  • 司卫少卿杨思俭女 - Yang Guifei
  • 元爽子承嗣 - Li Yuanxiang
  • 上元元年 - The first year of the Shangyuan era (760 CE)

[718]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 義陽宣城公主 - Princesses Yiyang and Xuancheng, daughters of Empress Xiao, who were favored by Empress Wu.
  • 幽掖庭幾四十不嫁 - The princesses were confined in the palace for nearly forty years without being married, reflecting the political intrigue and power struggles of the time.
  • 太子弘言於帝 - Prince Hong, the eldest son of Emperor Gaozong, who was a potential heir.
  • 後怒，鴆殺弘 - Empress Wu's ruthless elimination of potential rivals, including her own son.
  • 帝將下詔遜位於後 - Emperor Gaozong's intention to abdicate in favor of Empress Wu, indicating her growing influence.
  • 宰相郝處俊固諫 - Hao Chujun, a loyal minister who opposed Empress Wu's ambitions.
  • 帝病頭，眩不能視 - Emperor Gaozong's illness, which provided an opportunity for Empress Wu to gain more power.
  • 侍醫張文仲、秦鳴鶴曰 - Court physicians who were trusted by the emperor and had the authority to treat him.
  • 風上逆，砭血，頭可愈 - A medical treatment method involving bloodletting to cure headaches.
  • 後內幸帝疾得自專 - Empress Wu's secret hope that the emperor's illness would allow her to take full control of the government.
  • 醫議疾，烏可罪 - The defense of the doctors, emphasizing the importance of medical advice.
  • 醫一再刺 - The doctors' actions, which were successful in curing the emperor's illness.
  • 吾目明矣 - The emperor's recovery, which was seen as a divine sign.
  • 天賜我師 - Empress Wu's interpretation of the doctors' success as a divine favor.
  • 身負繒寶以賜 - Her generous reward to the doctors, showing her gratitude and power.
  • 帝崩，中宗即位，天後稱皇太后 - The succession of Emperor Zhongzong and Empress Wu's elevation to Empress Dowager.
  • 遺詔軍國大務聽參決 - The will that granted Empress Wu significant political power.
  • 嗣聖元年，太后廢帝為盧陵王 - Empress Wu's deposal of Emperor Zhongzong, marking the beginning of her rule.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 蕭妃女義陽宣城公主 - Princesses Yiyang and Xuancheng, daughters of Empress Xiao, who were favored by Empress Wu.
  • 幽掖庭幾四十不嫁 - The princesses were confined in the palace for nearly forty years without being married, reflecting the political intrigue and power struggles of the time.
  • 太子弘言於帝 - Prince Hong, the eldest son of Emperor Gaozong, who was a potential heir.
  • 後怒，鴆殺弘 - Empress Wu's ruthless elimination of potential rivals, including her own son.
  • 帝將下詔遜位於後 - Emperor Gaozong's intention to abdicate in favor of Empress Wu, indicating her growing influence.
  • 宰相郝處俊固諫 - Hao Chujun, a loyal minister who opposed Empress Wu's ambitions.
  • 帝病頭，眩不能視 - Emperor Gaozong's illness, which provided an opportunity for Empress Wu to gain more power.
  • 侍醫張文仲、秦鳴鶴曰 - Court physicians who were trusted by the emperor and had the authority to treat him.

  • 天賜我師 - Empress Wu's interpretation of the doctors' success as a divine favor.
  • 身負繒寶以賜 - Her generous reward to the doctors, showing her gratitude and power.
  • 帝崩，中宗即位，天後稱皇太后 - The succession of Emperor Zhongzong and Empress Wu's elevation to Empress Dowager.

[720]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 陛下 - A respectful term for the emperor.
  • 懷義有巧性 - Indicates that Huaiyi has a talent for craftsmanship or artistry.
  • 欲宫中驱使者 - Refers to the desire to use someone as a servant in the palace.
  • 臣请阉之 - Suggests that the subject is asking to have Huaiyi castrated, which was a common practice for eunuchs.
  • 庶不乱宫闱 - Means 'so that he will not disrupt the palace.'
  • 表寝不出 - Indicates that the proposal was not accepted or acted upon.
  • 堂成 - Refers to the completion of a hall or building.
  • 拜左威卫大将军梁国公 - Indicates a promotion to the rank of Left Vigilant General and the title of Duke of Liang.
  • 太后寻郊见上帝 - Means that the empress dowager performed a ritual sacrifice to the supreme deity.
  • 加尊号曰圣母神皇 - Indicates that the empress dowager received a new title.
  • 享万象神宫 - Refers to a ritual in the Temple of Ten Thousand Things.
  • 制曌等十二文 - Means that twelve new characters were created, one of which is 'zhao' (曌).
  • 自名为曌 - Indicates that the empress dowager chose this character as her name.
  • 进拜怀义辅国大将军鄂国公 - Suggests a further promotion for Huaiyi.
  • 令与群浮屠作大云经 - Means that Huaiyi was ordered to collaborate with Buddhist monks to create the *Da Yun Jing*.
  • 言神皇革命事 - Indicates that the *Da Yun Jing* discusses the empress dowager's revolution.
  • 颁示天下 - Means that the text was distributed throughout the empire.
  • 后稍图革命 - Suggests that the empress dowager began to plan a revolution.
  • 然虑人心不肯附 - Indicates that she was concerned about people's reluctance to support her.
  • 乃阴忍鸷害 - Suggests that she resorted to ruthless and secretive means.
  • 斩杀怖天下 - Means that she ordered executions to instill fear.
  • 内纵酷吏周兴、来俊臣等为爪吻 - Indicates that she allowed harsh officials like Zhou Xing and Lai Junchen to act as her enforcers.
  • 有不慊，若素疑惮者，必危法中之 - Means that anyone who displeased her or was suspected of disloyalty would be punished severely.
  • 宗姓侯王 - Refers to members of the imperial family.
  • 及他骨鲠臣将相 - Includes other upright officials and generals.

  • 太后操奁具坐重帏 - Means that the empress dowager sat behind a screen with her tools of governance.

  • 遂大赦天下 - Means that a general amnesty was granted.
  • 改国号周 - Refers to the change of the country's name to Zhou.
  • 自称圣神皇帝 - Means that she proclaimed herself the Holy and Divine Emperor.
  • 立武氏七庙 - Refers to the establishment of seven ancestral temples for the Wu family.
  • 皆尊帝号 - Means that all were given imperial titles.
  • 太子从姓武 - Refers to the crown prince adopting the Wu surname.
  • 降为皇嗣 - Means that he was demoted to the status of imperial heir.

[721]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 太后雖春秋高 - Though the太后is advanced in age (春秋, literally 'autumn and spring,' a euphemism for age).
  • 善自涂泽 - Skillfully takes care of herself or carefully maintains her appearance. '涂泽' refers to applying cosmetics or skincare.
  • 左右亦不觉其衰也 - Those around her also do not perceive her decline. '左右' refers to the people in attendance or close to her.
  • 俄而二齿生 - Soon after, two teeth grew. This is an unusual expression, possibly metaphorical or referring to a symbolic or miraculous event.
  • 下诏改元为寿 - She issued an edict to change the era name to 'Shou' (寿), meaning 'longevity' or 'life.' This is a common imperial practice to mark significant events.
  • 又自加号金轮圣神皇帝 - She also gave herself the title of 'Jinlun Shengshen Huangdi' (金轮圣神皇帝), meaning 'the Divine Emperor of the Golden Wheel.' This is a self-bestowed imperial title.
  • 置七宝于廷 - She placed the Seven Treasures in the court. '七宝' refers to a set of symbolic treasures.
  • 大朝会则陈之 - They were displayed during major court ceremonies. '陈' means to display or arrange.
  • 气盖一时 - His prestige dominated his time. This is a classical expression of great influence or status.
  • 出百官上 - He surpassed all the officials. This indicates his elevated position.
  • 明堂既成 - When the Mingtang (明堂) was completed. The Mingtang was a grand temple or hall used for rituals and state functions.
  • 太后命怀义作夹紵大像 - The太后ordered Huaiyi to create a large composite image made of lacquered silk. '夹紵' refers to a type of lacquered silk used for large-scale artistic works.
  • 其小指中犹容数十人 - Its little finger could still contain dozens of people. This is an exaggeration to emphasize the immense size of the image.
  • 于明堂北构天堂以贮之 - She built a 'Tian堂' (Heavenly Hall) to the north of the Mingtang to house it. '天堂' is a grand hall or temple, possibly with symbolic or religious significance.
  • 当始构，为风所摧 - When it was first constructed, it was destroyed by the wind. This suggests the structure was unstable or the project was repeated.
  • 日役万人 - Each day, ten thousand people were employed. This is a hyperbolic expression indicating massive labor force.
  • 采木江岭 - Timber was gathered from the rivers and mountains. This refers to the sourcing of materials from natural environments.
  • 数年之间，费以万亿计 - Within several years, the cost reached tens of millions. This is a classical way of expressing large sums.
  • 府藏为之耗竭 - The state treasury was thus exhausted. This indicates the financial strain of the project.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 太后 - Refers to the Empress Dowager, likely Wu Zetian (武则天), the only female emperor in Chinese history.
  • 春秋高 - A euphemistic way of referring to advanced age in classical Chinese.
  • 改元为寿 - A practice in Chinese imperial history where the emperor (or empress dowager) changes the era name to mark a significant event, often related to longevity or divine favor.
  • 金轮圣神皇帝 - A self-bestowed title of Wu Zetian, reflecting her claim to imperial authority and divine legitimacy.
  • 七宝 - The 'Seven Treasures' are a symbolic set of items used in imperial ceremonies, often representing wealth, power, and spiritual authority.
  • 明堂 - A grand hall used for state rituals, sacrifices, and political functions. It was a symbol of imperial legitimacy.
  • 夹紵大像 - A large-scale lacquered silk image, likely a Buddhist or imperial icon. This was a significant artistic and religious project.
  • 天堂 - A 'Heavenly Hall,' which may be a symbolic or actual building used to house the large image. It may also be a reference to a Buddhist concept of heaven or a symbolic structure.
  • 怀义 - Likely refers to Huaiyi (怀义), a Buddhist monk who was a close associate of Wu Zetian and held significant influence.
  • 府藏 - The state treasury or imperial coffers, indicating the vast resources used in the construction projects.
  • 费以万亿计 - A classical way of expressing large sums of money, often hyperbolic in nature.

Proper Nouns

  • 太后 - The Empress Dowager, likely Wu Zetian.
  • 怀义 - Huaiyi, a Buddhist monk and close associate of Wu Zetian.
  • 明堂 - The Mingtang, a grand hall for imperial rituals.
  • 金轮圣神皇帝 - A self-bestowed title of Wu Zetian.
  • 七宝 - The Seven Treasures, a symbolic set of items.
  • 金轮宝、白象宝、女宝、马宝、珠宝、主兵臣宝、主藏臣宝 - The individual components of the Seven Treasures.
  • 天堂 - The Heavenly Hall, a structure built to house the large image.
  • 府藏 - The imperial treasury.

[722]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 散钱十车 - Means 'to distribute ten carts of money,' indicating a large amount of money being given away.

[723]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 结彩为宫殿 - Literally 'to weave colors into palaces,' meaning to decorate or create elaborate structures with colorful materials.
  • 佛像皆于坑中引出之，云自池涌出 - Metaphorically describes how the Buddha statues were brought out of pits and said to emerge from a pool, suggesting a miraculous or divine origin.
  • 乃杀牛取血画大像，首高二百丈，云怀义刺膝血为之 - Describes a gruesome method of creating a large Buddha statue using the blood of a sacrificed cow, with the statue's head being 200 feet tall. The phrase '怀义刺膝血' (Huaiyi's knee-blood) is particularly unusual and may refer to a specific individual or event, but its exact meaning is unclear.
  • 张像于天津桥南，设斋 - '张像' means to display or set up the statue, and '设斋' refers to holding a vegetarian meal or ritual offering.
  • 怀义心愠 - '心愠' means to be angry or resentful, indicating that Huaiyi was displeased.
  • 是夕，密烧天堂，延及明堂，火照城中如昼，比明皆尽 - Describes a secret fire set at night that spread from the '天堂' (Tiantang, meaning 'Heavenly Hall') to the '明堂' (Mingtang, meaning 'Bright Hall'), illuminating the city like day and consuming everything by morning.
  • 暴风裂血像为数百段 - A violent storm shattered the blood-stained statue into hundreds of pieces.
  • 太后耻而讳之，但云内作工徒，误烧麻主，遂涉明堂 - The Empress Dowager was ashamed and tried to cover up the incident, claiming it was an accident caused by workers burning a '麻主' (má zhǔ), which could refer to a type of fabric or material. The phrase '遂涉明堂' suggests that the fire spread to the Bright Hall.
  • 命更造之，仍以怀义充使 - The Empress Dowager ordered the statue to be rebuilt and appointed Huaiyi to oversee the project.
  • 又铸铜为九州鼎及十二神，皆高一丈，各置其方 - This describes the casting of bronze statues representing the nine provinces and twelve deities, each standing one zhang (approximately 3.3 meters) tall, placed in their respective locations.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 天津桥 - A famous bridge in Luoyang during the Tang Dynasty, often associated with important events and ceremonies.
  • 明堂 - A grand hall used for imperial rituals and ceremonies, symbolizing the emperor's connection to heaven.
  • 太后 - Refers to the Empress Dowager, a powerful figure in the Tang court.
  • 怀义 - A monk or official whose name is mentioned in the text, but whose exact role is not clear. The phrase '怀义刺膝血' suggests he may have been involved in the creation of the statue in some way.
  • 九州鼎 - Bronze vessels representing the nine provinces of China, often used in rituals to symbolize unity and order.
  • 十二神 - Twelve deities or spirits, possibly representing the twelve directions or months of the year, commonly used in Chinese cosmology and ritual.

Proper Nouns

  • 天津桥 - A historical bridge in Luoyang.
  • 明堂 - A historical building in Luoyang used for imperial rituals.
  • 太后 - The Empress Dowager of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 怀义 - A historical figure, possibly a monk or official, whose exact role is not clear.
  • 九州鼎 - Historical bronze vessels representing the nine provinces of China.
  • 十二神 - Historical deities or spirits used in Chinese cosmology and ritual.

[724]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 先是 - Temporal phrase meaning 'previously' or 'earlier.'
  • 昼食一麻一米 - Means 'eating one sesame seed and one grain of rice during the day,' likely a reference to her ascetic lifestyle.
  • 夜则烹宰宴乐 - Means 'at night she would slaughter and feast,' indicating a contrast to her daytime asceticism.
  • 畜弟子百余人 - Means 'she had over a hundred disciples,' suggesting a large following.
  • 淫秽靡所不为 - Means 'she engaged in every kind of debauchery,' indicating her moral corruption.
  • 武什方自言能合长生药 - Means 'Wu Shifang claimed he could concoct the elixir of immortality,' referring to a Taoist alchemist.
  • 遣乘驿于岭南采药 - Means 'sent him by post to Lingnan to gather herbs,' indicating the imperial court's interest in his alchemical skills.
  • 及明堂火 - Means 'when the Hall of Brightness burned down,' referring to a significant event.
  • 尼入唁 - Means 'the nun came to offer condolences,' indicating her presence at the scene.
  • 汝常言能前知 - Means 'you often said you could foresee the future,' referring to the nun's supposed clairvoyance.
  • 何以不言明堂火 - Means 'why didn't you predict the fire at the Hall of Brightness?' questioning her clairvoyance.
  • 斥还河内 - Means 'she was dismissed and sent back to Hedong,' indicating her expulsion from the capital.
  • 弟子及老尼等皆逃散 - Means 'her disciples and the old nun all fled,' indicating the collapse of her following.
  • 发其奸者 - Means 'those who exposed her corruption,' referring to individuals who revealed her immoral behavior.
  • 复召尼还麟趾寺 - Means 'she was recalled to the Linzhi Temple,' indicating her return to the capital.
  • 敕给使掩捕 - Means 'imperial officials were ordered to arrest her,' indicating the imperial court's intervention.
  • 尽获之，皆没为官婢 - Means 'they were all captured and made into official slaves,' indicating the severe punishment.
  • 什方闻之自缢死 - Means 'Wu Shifang heard about it and hanged himself,' indicating his suicide.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sequence must be read as Ren Jie removing his fur coat before the emperor, bowing in gratitude, and leaving.

  • This sequence must be read as Ren Jie dying, and Changzong's brothers becoming more arrogant.
  • This sequence must be read as the Empress Dowager growing old and weary of politics, often leaving it to others.
  • This sequence must be read as Princess Shao's son Zhongrun and his sister Princess Yongtai, along with her husband Prince Wei Wu Yanji, secretly discussing the matter.
  • This sequence must be read as Yizhi reporting to the Empress Dowager.
  • This sequence must be read as they were all forced to commit suicide.
  • This sequence must be read as Yanji was the son of Chengsi.
  • This sequence must be read as soon after, Sili Shaoqing Tongxiu, along with Changzong's brother Cai Zhou Zhishi Changqi and his younger brother Shangfang Shaomin Changyi, were all imprisoned for corruption and bribery.
  • This sequence must be read as ordered both the left and right tribunals to jointly interrogate them.
  • This sequence must be read as soon after, he ordered Yizhi and Changzong to exercise power and privilege, and also ordered them to join the interrogation.
  • This sequence must be read as The Chief Censor Li Chengjia and others reported that Zhang Tongxiu's brothers had embezzled over four thousand strings of cash.
  • This sequence must be read as Zhang Changzong should be stripped of his office according to the law.
  • This sequence must be read as Changzong argued, 'I have served the state, and the law does not warrant removing my office.'
  • This sequence must be read as The Empress Dowager asked the chancellors, 'Has Changzong served the state?'
  • This sequence must be read as Yang Zaisi said, 'Changzong has prepared the elixir of immortality, which has proven effective when the Empress Dowager took it. This is the greatest service to the state.'
  • This sequence must be read as The Empress Dowager was pleased and pardoned Changzong, restoring his office.
  • This sequence must be read as Tongxiu was demoted to the position of magistrate of Qishan, and Changyi was demoted to the position of magistrate of Bowang.
  • This sequence must be read as Not long after, they were reinstated.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 太后寝疾 - Literally 'the Empress Dowager was ill,' implying she was gravely ill or on her deathbed.
  • 居长生院 - Means 'resided in the Longsheng Hall,' a palace hall where she was staying.
  • 宰相不得见者累月 - Means 'the prime minister was not allowed to see her for many months,' indicating a period of isolation.
  • 惟张易之、昌宗侍疾 - Means 'only Zhang Yizhi and Changzong attended to her illness,' highlighting their privileged position.
  • 少间 - Means 'a little while later' or 'after a short time.'
  • 崔玄暐奏言 - Means 'Cui Xuanwei made a report,' indicating a formal address.
  • 皇太子、相王，仁明孝友，足侍汤药 - Means 'the Crown Prince and Prince Xiang are benevolent, wise, filial, and kind, and are capable of attending to the Empress Dowager's needs.'
  • 宫禁事重，伏愿不令异姓出入 - Means 'the palace is a place of great importance, I humbly request that no outsiders be allowed to enter.'
  • 太后曰：“德卿厚意” - Means 'the Empress Dowager said, 'Deqing has good intentions.'
  • 易之、昌宗见太后疾笃，恐祸及己，引用党援，阴为之备 - Means 'Zhang Yizhi and Changzong, seeing the Empress Dowager's condition was serious, feared they would be implicated and began to gather allies and prepare secretly.'
  • 屡有人为飞书，及榜其书于通衢云：“易之兄弟谋反” - Means 'many times people wrote anonymous letters, and posted them in public places saying, 'The brothers Zhang Yizhi are plotting a rebellion.'
  • 太后皆不问 - Means 'the Empress Dowager did not inquire into these matters.'
  • 明年正月，赦天下，改元 - Means 'in the following year's first month, a general amnesty was declared, and the era name was changed.'
  • 太后疾益甚，惟二张居中用事 - Means 'the Empress Dowager's condition worsened further, and only the two Zhangs held power.'
  • 宰相张柬之等定计，率飞骑五百人，至东宫，迎皇太子至玄武门，斩关而入，诛昌宗、易之于庑下 - Means 'Prime Minister Zhang Jianzhi and others devised a plan, leading five hundred elite cavalry to the Eastern Palace, where they escorted the Crown Prince to Xuanwu Gate, broke through the gates, and killed Changzong and Yizhi in the portico.'
  • 是日，悉诛张昌期等 - Means 'on that day, all the Zhangs were executed.'
  • 太后传位太子 - Means 'the Empress Dowager abdicated the throne to the Crown Prince.'
  • 韦后居中宫 - Means 'Empress Wei resided in the central palace.'
  • 上官昭容与政事 - Means 'Shangguan Zhaorong participated in state affairs.'
  • 敬晖等将尽诛诸武 - Means 'Jing Hui and others intended to exterminate all the Wu clan.'
  • 武三思惧，乃因昭容入请，得幸于后，卒谋晖等诛之 - Means 'Wu Sansi, fearing for his life, used Shangguan Zhaorong to gain favor with the Empress, and eventually plotted to have Jing Hui and others killed.'
  • 初帝幽废，与后约：“一朝见天日，不相制” - Means 'when the emperor was first imprisoned and deposed, he made an agreement with the Empress: 'If one day I see the light of day again, I will not control you.'
  • 至是，与三思叩御床博戏，帝从旁典筹，不为忤 - Means 'by then, he was playing dice with Wu Sansi, and the emperor, sitting beside them, did not object.'
  • 三思讽群臣上后号为顺天皇后 - Means 'Wu Sansi persuaded the ministers to give the Empress the title 'Empress of Heaven.'
  • 乃亲谒宗庙，赠父玄贞上洛郡王 - Means 'she then personally paid homage to the ancestral temples and posthumously honored her father Xuanzhen as the Prince of Shangluo.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 太后 - Refers to Wu Zetian, the Empress Dowager and later Empress of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 长生院 - A specific palace hall where the Empress Dowager stayed.
  • 宰相 - Refers to the prime minister, a high-ranking official in the Tang government.
  • 张易之、昌宗 - Two influential eunuchs during Wu Zetian's reign.
  • 崔玄暐 - A prominent minister who opposed the eunuchs.
  • 皇太子 - The Crown Prince, later Emperor Zhongzong of Tang.
  • 相王 - Prince Xiang, later Emperor Ruizong of Tang.
  • 韦后 - Empress Wei, wife of Emperor Zhongzong.
  • 上官昭容 - Shangguan Zhaorong, a powerful female official during Wu Zetian's reign.
  • 敬晖 - A loyalist minister who opposed the eunuchs and the Wu clan.
  • 武三思 - A member of the Wu clan and a powerful figure during Wu Zetian's reign.
  • 顺天皇后 - A title given to Empress Wei.
  • 上洛郡王 - A posthumous title given to Empress Wei's father.

Proper Nouns

  • 太后 - Wu Zetian.
  • 长生院 - A specific palace hall.
  • 宰相 - Prime minister.
  • 张易之、昌宗 - Eunuchs.

  • 天后世歌《武（娬）媚娘》 - Refers to a song popular during Empress Wu's reign.
  • 皇帝受命歌《英王石州》 - Refers to a song popular during the current emperor's reign.

  • 盖后妃之德专蚕桑，共宗庙事也 - Explains the empress's role in silkworm breeding and ancestral temple affairs.
  • 乃赐志忠第一区，彩七百段 - Jie Zhi Zhong was bestowed the first district and seven hundred pieces of silk.
  • 三年，帝亲郊，引后亚献 - The emperor personally performed the sacrifice and the empress served as the secondary offering.
  • 明年正月望夜，帝与后微服过市，徜徉观览，纵宫女出游，皆淫奔不还 - Describes the emperor and empress visiting the market in disguise, allowing palace women to go out, and the resulting promiscuous behavior.
  • 国子祭酒叶静能善禁戒 - Introduces the Master of the Imperial College named Ye Jingneng, who was skilled in prohibition.

  • 尝免丧，不历旬辄起 - The emperor rose from mourning within ten days.

  • 微临淄仗义 - Means 'were it not for Linzi's righteousness,' referring to the actions of Linzi that prevented a worse outcome.
  • 李其为韦乎 - Is a rhetorical question asking if Li (likely referring to Li Longji, the future Emperor Xuanzong) would have acted like Wei (Empress Wei).
  • 复依稀武、韦故辙也 - Means 'again following the old paths of Wu and Wei,' suggesting that Yang Guifei's behavior mirrored that of Wu Zetian and Empress Wei.
  • 吾独怪 - Means 'I alone find it strange,' expressing the author's surprise.
  • 尝误堕池中 - Means 'once accidentally fell into a pool.'

  • 上自执丽水镇库紫磨金琢成步摇 - Means 'the emperor personally held a purple gold hair ornament crafted from the treasure of Lishui.'
  • 至妆阁亲与插鬓 - Means 'and personally placed it in her hair at the dressing room,' indicating a personal gesture.
  • 朕得贵妃，如得至宝也 - Means 'I have gained the Concubine Yang, as if I have acquired a priceless treasure,' indicating his high regard.

  • 明皇之良心，未嘗死也 - Suggests that Emperor Xuanzong still had a sense of conscience; rendered to clarify that he still had a conscience.
  • 令姚、宋、韓、張諸公而在 - Hypothetical scenario where prominent ministers were still alive; rendered to maintain the conditional nature.
  • 烏有是哉 - Rhetorical question meaning 'how could there be such a thing?'; rendered to maintain its forceful tone.

[735]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 子猶氏曰 - Conventional citation form indicating the author or commentator, 'Zi You said' or 'Zi You remarked.'
  • 明皇一日杀三子 - Hyperbolic expression emphasizing the scale of the tragedy, 'the Ming Huang Emperor killed three sons in one day.'
  • 于亲生儿如刈草菅 - Metaphor comparing the emperor's treatment of his own sons to cutting down weeds, indicating extreme cruelty.
  • 呼胡人为儿 - Derogatory expression implying a lack of familial affection, 'calling the barbarian a son.'
  • 乃望其孝顺乎 - Rhetorical question expressing doubt, 'how could one expect him to be filial?'
  • 禄山在旁而扪宠妃之乳 - Vivid and shocking description of An Lushan touching the emperor's favorite concubine's breast, indicating inappropriate behavior.
  • 与为调谑 - Suggesting a lack of decorum, 'teasing and joking with each other.'
  • 固已自诲之淫矣 - Indicating moral corruption, 'he has already been teaching himself debauchery.'
  • 禄山母贵妃而私之 - Scandalous claim, 'An Lushan's mother was the favorite concubine and he had private relations with her.'
  • 独无罪乎 - Rhetorical question expressing disbelief, 'is he alone without sin?'
  • 胡俗：父死则妻其母 - Cultural practice among the barbarians where a son marries his stepmother after his father's death.
  • 禄山特预为之耳 - Suggesting premeditation, 'An Lushan was just preparing for it.'
  • 且贵妃固明皇真子妇也 - Indicating a familial relationship, 'and the favorite concubine was indeed the true daughter-in-law of the Ming Huang Emperor.'
  • 真子妇可妻，于假母何有焉 - Rhetorical question expressing moral ambiguity, 'the true daughter-in-law can be married, but what does it matter to the false mother?'
  • 寿王之恨，报在禄山 - Indicating a cycle of retribution, 'the hatred of the Prince of Shou will be avenged by An Lushan.'
  • 明皇之疑妃，而终不能发，中有不慊故也 - Describing unresolved tension and underlying issues, 'the Ming Huang Emperor suspected the favorite concubine but ultimately could not expose it, due to underlying dissatisfaction.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 明皇 - Refers to Emperor Xuanzong of Tang (685–762), also known as Ming Huang, who reigned during the peak of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 贵妃 - Refers to Yang Guifei, the favorite concubine of Emperor Xuanzong, known for her beauty and influence.
  • 寿王 - Refers to Prince Shou, a son of Emperor Xuanzong, who was married to Yang Guifei before she became the emperor's concubine.
  • 胡人 - A term used to refer to non-Han Chinese people, often with a negative connotation.
  • 胡俗 - Refers to the customs and practices of the barbarians, often viewed negatively by the Han Chinese.

Proper Nouns

  • 明皇 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.

  • 寿王 - Prince Shou, a son of Emperor Xuanzong.
  • 胡人 - Non-Han Chinese people.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 吏部侍郎韩昭，字德华，长安人 - Han Zhao was a high-ranking official in the Jin dynasty, born in Chang’an (modern Xi’an). This establishes the historical and geographical context of the text.
  • 衍北巡，以为文思殿学士、京城留守判官 - ‘衍’ is likely a ruler or emperor, possibly a previous emperor of the Jin dynasty, who was on a northern tour and appointed Han Zhao to these positions.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 海陵嬖寵愈多 - 海陵 refers to Emperor Wu of Jin (Wu Zetian), who had many favorites. 嬖寵 means favored concubines or mistresses.
  • 定哥希得見 - 希 means rarely, and 得見 means to be seen or to have an audience. Together, it means that Ding Gao rarely got to see the emperor.
  • 號呼求去，诅骂海陵 - 號呼 means to shout or cry out, and 求去 means to request departure. 诅骂 means to curse or abuse. Together, it means that Ding Gao shouted, requested to leave, and cursed Emperor Wu.
  • 海陵阳为不闻而去 - 阳为不闻 means to pretend not to hear, and 而去 means to leave. Together, it means that Emperor Wu pretended not to hear and left.
  • 与家奴阎乞儿通 - 通 means to have a relationship or connection, often implying an illicit one. Here, it means that Ding Gao had an affair with a slave named Yan Qier.
  • 尝以衣服遗乞儿 - 尝 means to give or bestow, and 衣服 means clothes. Together, it means that Ding Gao often gave clothes to Yan Qier.
  • 给事本位 - 给事 means to serve or work, and 本位 means one's own position or place. Together, it means that Yan Qier served in his own position within the palace.
  • 欲复与乞儿通 - 欲复 means to want to again, and 通 means to have a relationship or connection. Together, it means that Ding Gao wanted to resume her affair with Yan Qier.
  • 使比丘尼向乞儿索所遗衣服以调之 - 使 means to send, 比丘尼 means Buddhist nun, 向 means to go to, 索 means to ask for, 所遗 means the given, and 调之 means to tempt or seduce. Together, it means that Ding Gao sent a Buddhist nun to Yan Qier to ask for the clothes she had given him and to tempt him.
  • 笑曰：“妃今日富贵忘我耶！ - 笑曰 means to laugh and say, 妃 means empress, 今日 means today, 富贵 means wealth and honor, and 忘我耶 means have you forgotten me? Together, it means that Yan Qier laughed and said, 'Empress, have you forgotten me today because of your wealth and honor?'
  • 使人诘责阍者曰：“我天子妃，亲体之衣，尔故元视何也？我且奏之！ - 使人诘责 means to send someone to reprimand, 阍者 means gatekeeper, 我天子妃 means I am the empress, 亲体之衣 means my personal clothing, 尔故元视何也 means why did you not check, and 我且奏之 means I will report this. Together, it means that Ding Gao sent someone to reprimand the gatekeeper, saying, 'I am the empress, my personal clothing, why did you not check? I will report this.'
  • 阍者惶恐曰：“死罪。请后不敢！ - 惶恐 means to be terrified, 死罪 means a grave mistake, and 请后不敢 means please do not do it again. Together, it means that the gatekeeper was terrified and said, 'A grave mistake. Please do not do it again.'
  • 使人以箧盛乞儿，载入宫中 - 使人以箧盛 means to send someone to put in a box, 乞儿 means Yan Qier, and 载入宫中 means to bring into the palace. Together, it means that Ding Gao sent someone to put Yan Qier in a box and bring him into the palace.
  • 阍者果不敢复索 - 果 means indeed, and 不敢复索 means did not dare to search again. Together, it means that the gatekeeper indeed did not dare to search again.
  • 使衣妇人衣，杂诸宫婢，抵暮遣出 - 使衣妇人衣 means to dress in women's clothes, 杂诸宫婢 means among the palace maids, and 抵暮遣出 means until dusk and send out. Together, it means that Yan Qier was dressed in women's clothes, mixed among the palace maids, and sent out until dusk.
  • 贵哥以告海陵 - 贵哥 is another name for Ding Gao, 以告 means to report, and 海陵 means Emperor Wu. Together, it means that Guiguo reported this to Emperor Wu.
  • 定哥缢死 - 缢死 means to die by hanging. Together, it means that Ding Gao died by hanging.
  • 乞儿及比丘尼三人皆伏诛 - 伏诛 means to be executed. Together, it means that Yan Qier and the three Buddhist nuns were all executed.
  • 封贵哥萃莘国夫人 - 封 means to bestow, 贵哥 is another name for Ding Gao, and 萃莘国夫人 means Lady of Cuisen State. Together, it means that Guiguo was bestowed the title of Lady of Cuisen State.

  • 公固止之，将成疾矣 - Phrase suggesting that continuing would be harmful, meaning 'You should definitely stop him; otherwise, he will become ill.'

  • 诏尚书省，凡后妃有请于宰相者，收其使以闻 - Phrase indicating an official decree, meaning 'He issued an edict to the Shangshu Province, instructing that any requests from consorts to the chancellor be reported to him.'

  • 哈麻之妹婿，集贤学士秃鲁帖木儿，故有宠于帝 - Harach's brother-in-law, the Scholar-in-Attendance Tolu Tumur, was favored by the emperor.

  • 其僧善秘密法，谓帝曰 - This monk was skilled in secret methods and told the emperor.
  • 陛下虽尊居万乘，富有四海，不过保有见世而已 - Metaphor suggesting the impermanence of material wealth and power.

  • 曰演楪儿，曰秘密，皆房中术也 - Called Yanyan'er and Secret, they are all forms of sexual techniques.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 愿陛下时忘万事，养精游神 - A polite request for the emperor to occasionally forget worldly affairs and rest his spirit.
  • 从中掖庭回舆，枉路临妾山林 - A humble request for the emperor to take a detour from the imperial palace to visit the speaker's humble retreat.
  • 得献觞上寿，娱乐左右 - An expression of hope to offer a toast and entertain the emperor's attendants.
  • 如是而死，何恨之有 - A rhetorical question expressing that if the speaker could achieve this, they would have no regrets even if they were to die.
  • 主何忧？幸得愈 - A reassuring statement that the princess has no worries and has recovered.
  • 恐群臣从官多，大为主费 - An expression of concern that the presence of many officials would be costly to the princess.
  • 有顷，主疾愈起谒 - A statement that after a short while, the princess, having recovered, rose to pay respects.
  • 自执宰，敝膝道入，登阶就坐 - A description of the princess personally preparing the meal, removing her shoes, and ascending the steps to sit.
  • 愿思主人翁 - A request for the emperor to consider the princess as his host.
  • 去簪珥，徒跣顿首谢 - A gesture of deep respect where the princess removes her hair ornaments, walks barefoot, and kowtows.
  • 身当伏诛，陛下不致之法，顿首死罪 - A statement of the princess's willingness to face punishment, but the emperor's mercy spares her.
  • 有诏谢主，簪屐起，之东厢，自引董君 - The emperor's response, where he thanks the princess, puts on his hat and shoes, and invites Dong Jun to join him.
  • 董君绿帻傅鞲，随主前，伏殿下 - A description of Dong Jun following the princess, wearing a green cap and holding a bellows, bowing before the emperor.
  • 主乃赞：“馆陶公主庖人臣偃，昧死再拜谒。” - The princess introduces Dong Jun as her cook, prostrating herself twice in a show of utmost respect.
  • 因叩头谢 - Dong Jun also kowtows in gratitude.
  • 上为之起，有诏赐衣冠 - The emperor rises and orders that Dong Jun be given proper attire.
  • 主自奉食，进觞 - The princess serves the food and wine herself.
  • 饮大欢乐 - The scene is one of great joy and merriment.
  • 主乃请赐将军列侯从官金钱杂绘各有数 - The princess requests that the emperor grant gifts of money and silk to the generals, marquises, and officials, each according to their rank.
  • 于是董君贵宠，天下莫不闻 - As a result, Dong Jun gains great favor and becomes widely known.
  • 郡国狗马、蹴鞠、剑客，辐辏董氏 - People from various regions, including dog and horse breeders, football players, and swordsmen, flock to the Dong family.
  • 常从游献北宫，驰逐平乐，观鸡踘之会，角狗马之足 - They often accompany the emperor on his visits to the northern palace, racing and playing football, and participating in dog and horse competitions.
  • 上大欢乐之 - The emperor greatly enjoys these activities.
  • 于是上为窦太主置酒宣室，使谒者引内董君 - The emperor then holds a banquet for Princess Duan at the Xuan Room and has an usher bring Dong Jun in.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 是时 - Indicates a specific time or occasion, often used to set the scene.
  • 备戟殿下 - Refers to standing guard with a spear at the imperial palace, indicating a position of readiness or alertness.
  • 辟戟而前 - Literally means 'to part the spears and advance,' which is a metaphorical expression indicating that Eastern朔 stepped forward to speak.
  • 有斩罪三 - Refers to having three capital offenses, a serious accusation.
  • 富于春秋 - Means 'young and inexperienced,' often used to describe a young ruler.
  • 积思于六经 - Means 'to deeply contemplate the Six Classics,' referring to the foundational texts of Confucianism.
  • 留神于王事 - Means 'to pay close attention to state affairs.'
  • 驰骛于唐虞 - Refers to emulating the virtues of the legendary rulers Tang and Yu, who were known for their wise governance.
  • 折节于三代 - Means 'to humble oneself and learn from the Three Dynasties' (Xia, Shang, and Zhou), emphasizing the importance of historical precedent.
  • 靡丽为右 - Means 'to favor luxury and beauty,' indicating a preference for superficial and materialistic values.
  • 奢侈为务 - Means 'to make luxury a primary concern,' highlighting the pursuit of excessive wealth and enjoyment.
  • 尽狗马之乐 - Means 'to indulge in the pleasures of dogs and horses,' referring to the enjoyment of hunting and other recreational activities.
  • 极耳目之欲 - Means 'to satisfy every sensory desire,' indicating a life of indulgence.
  • 行邪枉之道 - Means 'to follow crooked and unjust paths,' suggesting immoral behavior.
  • 经淫僻之路 - Means 'to travel on the path of debauchery and perversion,' further emphasizing immoral and decadent behavior.
  • 国家之大贼 - Means 'a great enemy of the state,' indicating someone who poses a significant threat to the nation.
  • 人主之大蜮 - Means 'a great pest to the ruler,' suggesting a significant threat to the emperor.
  • 淫首 - Means 'leader of debauchery,' indicating the person who leads others into immoral behavior.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 东方朔 - A famous court jester during the Han Dynasty, known for his wit and wisdom.
  • 董偃 - A historical figure who was accused of improper behavior with the princess.
  • 六经 - The Six Classics, which are the foundational texts of Confucianism, including the *Book of Changes* (Yijing), *Book of Documents* (Shujing), *Book of Songs* (Shijing), *Book of Rites* (Liji), *Book of History* (Shi), and *Spring and Autumn Annals* (Chunqiu).
  • 唐虞 - The legendary rulers Tang and Yu, who were known for their wise governance and moral integrity.
  • 三代 - The Xia, Shang, and Zhou dynasties, which are often cited as models of good governance and moral leadership.
  • 靡丽 - Refers to a preference for luxury and beauty, often associated with superficial and materialistic values.
  • 奢侈 - Refers to excessive wealth and enjoyment, often seen as a vice.
  • 狗马之乐 - Refers to the enjoyment of hunting and other recreational activities, often associated with the nobility.
  • 耳目之欲 - Refers to the satisfaction of sensory desires, often seen as a form of indulgence.
  • 邪枉 - Refers to crooked and unjust paths, indicating immoral behavior.
  • 淫僻 - Refers to debauchery and perversion, further emphasizing immoral and decadent behavior.
  • 国家之大贼 - Refers to a great enemy of the state, indicating someone who poses a significant threat to the nation.
  • 人主之大蜮 - Refers to a great pest to the ruler, suggesting a significant threat to the emperor.
  • 淫首 - Refers to a leader of debauchery, indicating the person who leads others into immoral behavior.

  • 唐虞 - The legendary rulers Tang and Yu.

  • 吾业以设饮，后而自改 - Statement meaning 'I have already set up the banquet, and I will correct myself afterward.'

  • 非法度之政，不得入焉 - Statement meaning 'Without the authority of law and regulation, one cannot enter.'
  • 放淫乱之渐，其变为篡 - Statement meaning 'The gradual spread of debauchery will turn into usurpation.'
  • 竖貂为淫，而易牙作患 - Statement meaning 'Shudi was debauched, and Yiyá caused trouble.'
  • 庆父诛，而鲁国全；管蔡诛，而周室安 - Statement meaning 'When Qingfu was executed, the state of Lu was preserved; when Guan and Cai were executed, the Zhou dynasty was stabilized.'
  • 有诏止，更置酒北宫 - Statement meaning 'There was an imperial edict to stop, and instead, wine was served in the northern palace.'

  • 董君之宠，由是日衰 - Statement meaning 'Dong Jun's favor gradually declined.'
  • 至年三十而终 - Statement meaning 'He died at the age of thirty.'
  • 后数岁，窦太主卒，与董君合葬于霸陵 - Statement meaning 'After several years, Princess Dou died, and she was buried with Dong Jun in the Baling tomb.'
  • 是后公主贵人多逾礼制，自董偃始 - Statement meaning 'After this, many princesses and noblewomen exceeded the proper etiquette, starting with Dong Yan.'

  • 若河间何 - Reflects the common theme in classical Chinese literature of dealing with a virtuous or influential figure.

  • 车缕造门邀之遨嬉 - Reflects the use of flattery and luxury to try to win over a virtuous figure.
  • 美其辞曰 - Reflects the use of sweet talk and flattery in classical Chinese culture.
  • 自吾里有河间，戚里之人日夜为饬励 - Reflects the idea that the presence of a virtuous figure can inspire others to improve themselves.

  • 五凤 - Five phoenixes, a symbol of great prosperity and harmony. In Chinese culture, the phoenix is a symbol of the empress and represents grace, beauty, and longevity.
  • 随方之色 - Colors that follow the directions, likely referring to the colors of the five elements (wood, fire, earth, metal, water). This concept is rooted in traditional Chinese cosmology.
  • 龙山、龟山、凤山 - Names of mountains that take the shapes of dragons, turtles, and phoenixes, respectively. These mountains are often associated with specific virtues and are found in various Chinese legends.
  • 太白星 - The planet Venus, often associated with the god of war and metal. In Chinese mythology, Venus is linked to various deities and celestial beings.
  • 织女侍儿梁玉清、卫承庄 - Servants of the Weaving Maid (Venus), named Liang Yuqing and Wei Chengzhuang. The Weaving Maid is a prominent figure in Chinese folklore, known for her role in the legend of the Cowherd and the Weaver Girl.
  • 少仙洞 - A mythical cave where immortals or deities reside. Such caves are common in Chinese mythology and often serve as places of spiritual significance.
  • 天帝 - The supreme deity in Chinese mythology, often referred to as the Heavenly Emperor. The Heavenly Emperor is the ultimate authority in the celestial hierarchy.
  • 五岳 - The five sacred mountains in China, each associated with a cardinal direction and a specific color. These mountains are revered in Chinese culture and are often the subjects of religious and literary works.
  • 河伯 - The god of the Yellow River, often associated with water and fertility. River gods play important roles in Chinese mythology, particularly in stories related to agriculture and natural disasters.
  • 常春 - A place in the celestial realm, often associated with spring and renewal. This concept reflects the Chinese belief in the cyclical nature of the seasons and the importance of rebirth.
  • 独异志 - A collection of unusual stories and legends. This text is a valuable source for understanding the cultural and mythological landscape of ancient China.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sequence '少简贵，有清标，姿度美秀，善谈论，工草隶' describes Guo Han's character and abilities, and must be read as a single descriptive clause.
  • The sequence '早孤，独处，乘月卧庭中' describes Guo Han's background and daily routine, and must be read as a single descriptive clause.
  • The sequence '时时有微风，稍闻香气渐浓' describes the environmental changes, and must be read as a single descriptive clause.
  • The sequence '翰甚怪之，仰视空中，见有人冉冉而下，直至翰前，乃一少女也' describes Guo Han's reaction and the appearance of the girl, and must be read as a single narrative clause.
  • The sequence '明艳绝代，光彩溢目，衣玄绡之衣，曳罗霜之帔，戴翠翘凤凰之冠，蹑琼文九章之履' describes the girl's appearance, and must be read as a single descriptive clause.
  • The sequence '侍女二人，皆有殊色，感荡心神' describes the maidservants, and must be read as a single descriptive clause.
  • The sequence '翰整衣巾下床拜谒，不意尊灵迥降，愿垂德音' describes Guo Han's respectful gesture and his request, and must be read as a single narrative clause.
  • The sequence '吾天上织女也，久无主对，而嘉期阻旷，幽态盈怀，上帝赐命而游人间' describes the girl's identity and her reasons for visiting the mortal world, and must be read as a single narrative clause.
